Task id: T87 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: 817dac4 (main) — your worktree may be cut from an older HEAD; FIRST run `git reset --hard 817dac4` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T87` so your commit lands on the task branch.

NOTE: Go repository (wanbond). Gate: `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c` (no bun).

CONTEXT: T83-T86 have landed in internal/bind — peerState owns virt/scheduler/reflector/probers/sendCodec/paths/outerSeq/resequencer/fecSend/fecRecv (per-peer psk since T84); Send routes via peerByVirt (T85); the single ReceiveFunc drains every bound peer's resequencer (T86); Multipath embeds the primary *peerState.

headline: Preserve per-peer resequencer lifecycle and D32 rebaseline

description: Make the per-Open (re)creation of the resequencer and fecSend/fecRecv, and the SetPeerRemote/Rebaseline D32 fix, operate per peerState rather than process-globally: a rebaseline or reconnect on one peer must never touch another peer's release point. Keep the disjoint-mutex, never-held-across-syscall discipline per peer. (Check how Open currently rebuilds resequencer/fec state — if it only rebuilds the primary's via promotion, extend it to every bound peer; check SetPeerRemote's Rebaseline scope — it must re-baseline ONLY the peer whose remote changed. SetPeerRemote currently operates on the primary via promotion — decide and implement the per-peer form consistent with how the device will call it for a given peer.)

acceptance: Per-peer resequencer unit test: two interleaved outer-seq streams stay separated across an Open cycle; a Rebaseline() triggered on peer A leaves peer B's `next`/release point untouched (the D32-class regression, now per-peer). `go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/reseq/...` passes.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"crypto/rand"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"net"
8		"net/netip"
9		"sync"
10		"sync/atomic"
11		"syscall"
12		"time"
13	
14		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/conn"
15	
16		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
17		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
18		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
19		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
20		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/reseq"
21		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
22		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
23	)
24	
25	// socketRecvBuffer is the SO_RCVBUF requested on every per-path socket, matching
26	// wireguard-go's StdNetBind (~7 MiB). A large receive buffer absorbs the bursts a
27	// bonded, reordered flow delivers while the userspace engine catches up; the
28	// pass-through P0 Bind used the OS default and lost datagrams under load (P0
29	// findings §1/§2). The kernel silently caps the request at net.core.rmem_max, so
30	// the effective size may be smaller on an untuned host — best-effort by design.
31	const socketRecvBuffer = 7 << 20
32	
33	// multipathBatchSize is the number of datagrams a ReceiveFunc / Send handles per
34	// call. T12 keeps it at 1 (one syscall per datagram, per path); GSO/GRO batching
35	// is best-effort future work (P0 findings §2) tracked separately and does not
36	// change the wire format.
37	const multipathBatchSize = 1
38	
39	// maxDatagram bounds a single received outer datagram. It comfortably exceeds a
40	// full-MTU inner packet plus every outer/WG/amnezia-junk overhead.
41	const maxDatagram = 65535
42	
43	// Receive-resequencer tuning (T18), sized against P0 findings §6.
44	//
45	// resequencerWindow is the maximum span of outer-seq positions the receive
46	// resequencer buffers while it waits for an out-of-order frame. WG's inner RFC
47	// 6479 anti-replay window is 8128 messages (docs/p0-findings.md §6); the window
48	// here sits ~4x below that, so even a worst-case burst of reordered/held frames
49	// stays comfortably inside the inner filter's tolerance while spanning far more
50	// packets than the measured ~19 ms emulated cross-path skew (and the larger,
51	// more variable real Starlink/5G skew) needs at realistic per-path rates.
52	//
53	// resequencerTimeout bounds how long a head-of-line-blocked run is held for a
54	// missing (presumed-lost) lower frame before that gap is skipped and the run
55	// released. It caps the added latency — and the stall a slow/lossy path can
56	// impose on the whole bond (P0 §7 head-of-line concern) — to a few multiples of
57	// a Starlink RTT (~45 ms) rather than holding a gap forever.
58	const (
59		resequencerWindow  = 2048
60		resequencerTimeout = 250 * time.Millisecond
61	)
62	
63	var (
64		errNoHealthyPath = errors.New("bind: no healthy path with a known remote endpoint")
65		// errPacerShedding is returned by Send when the scheduler shed the datagram for
66		// pacing (PickPaced) while paths are healthy — deliberate rate limiting, NOT an
67		// outage. It is DISTINCT from errNoHealthyPath so operator logs and the e2e
68		// log-grep harness can tell shedding from total path failure (the drop behavior is
69		// identical to the pre-existing no-path case; only the diagnostic differs). The
70		// coalesced, rate-limited "pacer shedding" record is emitted by the scheduler.
71		errPacerShedding = errors.New("bind: datagram shed by send pacer (paths healthy, rate limited)")
72		errClosed        = net.ErrClosed
73	)
74	
75	// sharedPathState is the per-SOCKET state of one configured uplink, SHARED across
76	// every peer bound beneath the Bind: its stable path-id, source address, and the
77	// source-bound UDP socket (drained by exactly one Bind-owned readLoop). A concentrator
78	// front-ends many peers on the SAME socket, so this identity is owned once per socket
79	// and referenced by every peer's per-(peer,path) view (peerPathState). On the single-peer
80	// edge/hub there is exactly one peer, so each shared path has exactly one peerPathState.
81	// The deferred-path machinery (deferredPath, m.deferred) likewise operates on this shared
82	// socket layer — a runtime add/remove of a shared path fans the per-(peer,path) state out
83	// to every bound peer (see attachSharedPathLocked / RemovePath).
84	type sharedPathState struct {
85		name string
86		id   uint8
87		src  netip.Addr
88		conn *net.UDPConn
89	}
90	
91	// peerPathState is one peer's per-(peer,path) VIEW of a shared uplink: that peer's own
92	// decode Codec (each path receives on its own goroutine, so the Codec's scratch is never
93	// shared), the peer's own learned/configured return remote, the peer's own probe
94	// initiator for this path, and the peer's own per-path OUTER-wire byte counters. The
95	// remote is either configured (edge dest_addr / peer endpoint) or LEARNED from inbound
96	// traffic (concentrator) — but it lives strictly BELOW the engine's single virtual
97	// endpoint, so the engine never sees this per-(peer,path) bookkeeping churn.
98	//
99	// It EMBEDS its *sharedPathState so the socket identity (name/id/src/conn) is reached
100	// transparently, and back-references the owning peerState so a receive handler resolves
101	// exactly that peer's resequencer/reflector/FEC decoder.
102	type peerPathState struct {
103		*sharedPathState
104		peer  *peerState
105		codec *frame.Codec
106		// prober is this (peer,path)'s own probe initiator (nil when the bind runs without the
107		// probe transport). It is set at path creation and immutable for the path's life,
108		// so the Bind-owned receive goroutine reaches it via the peerPathState it already
109		// holds — NOT through a shared, dynamically-mutated probers slice — which is
110		// what keeps echo handling race-free while paths are added/removed at runtime (T30).
111		prober *telemetry.Prober
112	
113		// txBytes/rxBytes are cumulative OUTER-wire byte counters for this (peer,path), the
114		// per-path traffic accounting the /metrics exposition reports (T23). txBytes counts
115		// the DATA-frame wire bytes this path egresses on the Send hot path; rxBytes counts
116		// every outer datagram this path's readLoop receives (DATA, PROBE, and echo alike —
117		// the true received wire volume). They are atomics so the send/receive hot paths
118		// increment them WITHOUT taking m.mu (lock-free), and the scrape/snapshot path reads
119		// them with a plain atomic Load; a single writer per counter (Send holds m.mu while
120		// choosing the path, and each path has exactly one readLoop) means the Add is
121		// uncontended in the common case. "Bytes on Starlink vs 5G" — the data-thrift signal
122		// (requirement 2) — is exactly txBytes: when the weighted scheduler collapses to the
123		// primary under sub-capacity load, the backup path's Send count stays ~flat.
124		txBytes atomic.Uint64
125		rxBytes atomic.Uint64
126	
127		mu        sync.Mutex
128		remote    netip.AddrPort
129		hasRemote bool
130	}
131	
132	// peerState holds the per-PEER datapath state that was a process-global singleton before
133	// the multi-peer concentrator split: the SINGLE virtual endpoint the engine holds for this
134	// peer, the peer's send scheduler, its own outer-seq space, its shared send Codec, its
135	// probe reflector, its receive resequencer and FEC send/receive planes, its per-path probe
136	// initiators, and its per-(peer,path) views over the shared sockets. The single-peer
137	// edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split
138	// singleton — Multipath embeds it as the primary and the datapath reaches its fields through
139	// that embed); the concentrator constructs one peerState per bound peer. resequencer and
140	// fecRecv stay atomic.Pointer, and outerSeq atomic, so the lock-free receive/send fast paths
141	// read them WITHOUT m.mu.
142	type peerState struct {
143		// name is the peer id/name, the key under which Multipath.peersByName holds this peer.
144		// Empty on the single-peer edge/hub (there is only one peer to key).
145		name string
146	
147		// psk is THIS peer's effective pre-shared key: the sole seam from which every frame
148		// Codec this peer derives (its sendCodec and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via
149		// newCodec) and its probe Reflector authenticate. The single-peer edge/hub sets it to
150		// the one configured psk (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton);
151		// the concentrator sets a DIFFERENT psk per bound peer, so one peer's codec/reflector
152		// rejects another peer's frames (T84). NewCodec/NewReflector PSK-derivation itself is
153		// unchanged — only WHICH psk each peer feeds them differs.
154		psk config.Key
155	
156		// Immutable per-peer collaborators, built at construction and persisting across the
157		// Open→Close socket lifecycle (the concentrator pins virt's destination once for the
158		// process life, so virt in particular must NOT be recreated per Open).
159		virt      *udpEndpoint
160		scheduler sched.Scheduler
161		reflector *telemetry.Reflector
162		// newProber mints a prober for a path admitted to THIS peer at runtime (T30); nil
163		// disables runtime path addition for the peer (a bind without the probe transport).
164		newProber ProberFactory
165		// probers is this peer's BOOT-TIME per-path probe initiator set, in durable-membership
166		// (m.defs) order — bound AND deferred — shared with this peer's scheduler. At Open each
167		// bound entry is bound onto its peerPathState (pp.prober), and thereafter the hot paths
168		// reach a path's prober through the peerPathState — never by indexing this slice — so a
169		// runtime path add/remove cannot race echo handling. Nil when the peer runs without the
170		// probe transport; a nil here also gates the whole probe loop off.
171		probers []*telemetry.Prober
172	
173		// Per-Open state, (re)built by Open and cleared by Close.
174		sendCodec *frame.Codec
175		paths     []*peerPathState
176		outerSeq  atomic.Uint64
177		// resequencer is this peer's shared T18 receive resequencing buffer. Published
178		// atomically so the per-path readLoop goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu.
179		resequencer atomic.Pointer[reseq.Resequencer]
180		// FEC datapath (T24), per peer. fecSend accessed only under m.mu; fecRecv published
181		// atomically like resequencer. Both nil/empty when FEC is off.
182		fecSend *fecSender
183		fecRecv atomic.Pointer[fecReceiver]
184	}

186	// newPeerState builds the durable per-peer datapath state whose probe Reflector — and,
187	// through ps.psk, every frame Codec this peer later derives in Open/AddPath (its sendCodec
188	// and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via newCodec) — authenticate under THIS peer's
189	// psk. It is the seam that replaces the pre-split single-psk assumption (T84): the
190	// single-peer edge/hub mints exactly one (the primary) from the sole configured psk, while
191	// the concentrator mints one per bound peer from that peer's DIFFERENT effective psk, so a
192	// peerState built from a different psk gets its own codec/reflector and cross-psk frames are
193	// rejected. virt is created here (not per Open) because the concentrator pins its destination
194	// once for the process life. The Reflector draws its per-path challenges from crypto/rand,
195	// exactly as before. The per-Open fields (sendCodec, paths, resequencer, FEC planes) are
196	// left zero for Open to (re)build.
197	func newPeerState(name string, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, newProber ProberFactory, probers []*telemetry.Prober) *peerState {
198		return &peerState{
199			name:      name,
200			psk:       psk,
201			virt:      &udpEndpoint{},
202			scheduler: scheduler,
203			reflector: telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader),
204			newProber: newProber,
205			probers:   probers,
206		}
207	}

209	// newCodec derives a fresh frame Codec bound to THIS peer's psk — the send Codec at Open and
210	// each per-(peer,path) receive Codec share the derivation but not the instance, since a Codec
211	// is not safe for concurrent use (each receive path decodes on its own goroutine). Deriving
212	// from ps.psk (not a Multipath-wide key) is what makes a path's receive Codec the codec of the
213	// peer the path is bound to (T84).
214	func (ps *peerState) newCodec() (*frame.Codec, error) {
215		return frame.NewCodec(ps.psk)
216	}

218	// deferredPath is a configured path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr was not yet
219	// assignable at Open (net.ListenUDP -> EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the
220	// address, e.g. a 5G modem with no DHCP lease at boot). Rather than tear the whole
221	// bond down, Open records the path here instead of binding it: the tunnel comes up
222	// on the paths that DID bind, and this path stays DOWN — its prober, never fed an
223	// echo, reports StateDown, which the scheduler excludes from Pick, exactly as the
224	// runtime path-down model treats a live-but-silent path. It carries the boot-time
225	// prober so the T55 background reconcile that retries the bind reuses the SAME
226	// path-id stamp instead of minting a new one. prober is nil only on a bind without
227	// the probe transport, which never defers (see Open).
228	type deferredPath struct {
229		def    config.Path
230		prober *telemetry.Prober
231	}

245	// Multipath is the P1 bonding conn.Bind: one UDP socket per configured path
246	// (bound to the path's source address), all fronted by a SINGLE stable virtual
247	// endpoint the engine holds per peer. Send wraps each opaque WireGuard datagram
248	// in an outer DATA frame (own outer-seq + path-id) and picks a path; one
249	// Bind-owned reader per path unwraps DATA frames into the shared resequencer, and
250	// a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc drains it and hands the inner WG datagram up
251	// under the shared virtual endpoint (the fan-in that lets paths be added/removed at
252	// runtime without the engine spawning receive goroutines — T30). It replaces
253	// bind.Passthrough behind the same conn.Bind seam (device wiring is unchanged).
254	//
255	// Lifecycle: the sockets live for the duration of an Open→Close span, NOT the
256	// Multipath's whole life. The amneziawg engine calls Close() before every Open()
257	// (device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked, and on IpcSet listen_port /
258	// route-change events) and cycles Close↔Open on Down/Up, so — exactly like
259	// conn.StdNetBind — Open creates the per-path sockets and Close tears them down
260	// AND clears the path state so the next Open rebuilds from scratch. The "closed"
261	// state is simply "no bound sockets" (len(paths)==0); there is no separate sticky
262	// flag that a later Open would have to reset.
263	//
264	// Path policy: an injected sched.Scheduler (T15) chooses the egress path per
265	// datagram. The P1 MVP wires an active-backup scheduler (all traffic on the
266	// preferred primary, transparent failover to a backup on a T13 path-DOWN signal
267	// with failback hysteresis); the weighted/FEC-aware policy (T21) is a different
268	// Scheduler swapped in here with no Bind change. No FEC/resequencing yet
269	// (P2/P3), but every DATA frame carries its outer-seq and path-id so T18/T24 can
270	// consume them.
271	// ProberFactory mints a *telemetry.Prober for a path admitted at runtime (T30),
272	// stamped with the given stable path-id. The device wires it capturing the SAME
273	// per-boot session id, prober config, clock, PSK, and logger the boot-time probers
274	// were built with, so a runtime path's liveness is measured identically to a
275	// boot-time path (its probes join the same session so the peer's reflector adopts
276	// them without a challenge reset). It is nil on a bind built without the probe
277	// transport (the T12 unit tests), which therefore cannot add paths at runtime.
278	type ProberFactory func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober
279	
280	type Multipath struct {
281		defs []config.Path
282	
283		// classify maps each outbound datagram to its pacer traffic class from the inner
284		// WireGuard message type, parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia obfuscation profile
285		// so a WireGuard control frame (handshake/keepalive) is recognised — and pacing-
286		// exempted — under advanced security too, not only in vanilla mode (defect D22). It
287		// is immutable after construction and holds no lock, so Send reads it off m.mu.
288		classify wgClassifier
289	
290		// deferredListen binds a reconciled deferred path's socket (T55 background
291		// reconcile). It is an injection seam: the default pins the source IP (net.ListenUDP,
292		// matching AddPath's runtime bind), and a test overrides it to drive the
293		// deferred→bound transition deterministically — a source_addr "becoming assignable"
294		// — without a real interface address having to appear on the host. Immutable after
295		// construction, never nil.
296		deferredListen func(src netip.Addr, port uint16) (*net.UDPConn, error)
297	
298		mu sync.Mutex
299	
300		// The PRIMARY peer, embedded so the single-peer datapath (Send, the receive drainer,
301		// the probe loop) and the existing single-peer tests reach its fields — virt,
302		// scheduler, reflector, newProber, probers, sendCodec, paths, outerSeq, resequencer,
303		// fecSend/fecRecv — transparently through promotion, keeping behaviour byte-identical
304		// to the pre-split singleton. It is peers[0]; the concentrator's additional peers live
305		// in peers/peersByName. The former process-global resequencer/outerSeq/scheduler now
306		// live on this peerState, NOT on Multipath.
307		*peerState
308	
309		// peers is every bound peer (len 1 on the single-peer edge/hub). The runtime shared-
310		// path add/remove fan-out iterates it so per-(peer,path) state is created/torn-down for
311		// EVERY currently-bound peer (attachSharedPathLocked / RemovePath). peersByName keys
312		// them by peer id/name.
313		peers       []*peerState
314		peersByName map[string]*peerState
315		// peersView is the LOCK-FREE snapshot of the bound peer set the single engine-facing
316		// receive drainer iterates (newReceiveFunc). m.peers is mutated only under m.mu (peer
317		// wiring / fan-out); every mutation republishes this pointer (republishPeersLocked) so
318		// the drainer enumerates EVERY bound peer's resequencer WITHOUT taking m.mu on the
319		// receive hot path — the same lock-free-publish discipline resequencer/fecRecv use. It
320		// is never nil after construction (the constructor publishes the primary-only view).
321		peersView atomic.Pointer[[]*peerState]
322		// peerByEndpoint / peerBySource are the inbound demux lookup maps the concentrator's
323		// shared-socket receive path needs to route a datagram to the owning peer (endpoint or
324		// learned source → peer). They are PLACEHOLDERS today: the single-peer edge/hub needs no
325		// demux (one peer owns every socket), so they stay empty; the concentrator receive path
326		// (a later G4 task) populates and reads them.
327		peerByEndpoint map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState
328		peerBySource   map[netip.Addr]*peerState
329		// peerByVirt routes an OUTBOUND Send to its owning peer: the engine hands Send the
330		// single virtual endpoint (*udpEndpoint) it holds for a peer, and this map resolves
331		// that pointer to the peer's datapath state (outerSeq, scheduler, sendCodec, fecSend,
332		// per-(peer,path) set). It is the SEND-side dual of peerByEndpoint/peerBySource (which
333		// demux INBOUND datagrams). Each peer's virt is DISTINCT, so the lookup is exact; an
334		// endpoint not in this map is an unknown peer, which Send refuses rather than
335		// misrouting onto some other peer's paths. The primary is registered at construction;
336		// the concentrator registers each additional peer as it binds it. Read under m.mu on
337		// the Send path; written under m.mu (or at single-threaded construction).
338		peerByVirt map[*udpEndpoint]*peerState
339	
340		// shared is the SHARED per-socket path list (the sockets themselves), rebuilt from
341		// m.defs on every Open and mutated by the runtime add/remove. Each bound peer holds a
342		// peerPathState VIEW over a subset of these; on the single-peer edge/hub primary.paths
343		// is index-aligned with shared. len(shared)==0 is the "closed" (no sockets) state.
344		shared []*sharedPathState
345	
346		// deferred holds the configured paths whose well-formed source_addr was not yet
347		// assignable at the last Open (EADDRNOTAVAIL). They are NOT in shared or the
348		// scheduler — the tunnel runs on the paths that bound — but are recorded here,
349		// index-independent of shared, for the T55 background reconcile to retry as their
350		// addresses appear. Rebuilt from scratch on every Open; guarded by m.mu. The deferred-
351		// path machinery is SHARED (per-socket): a promoted deferred path fans its per-(peer,
352		// path) state out to every peer exactly as a runtime AddPath does.
353		deferred []deferredPath
354		// defaultRemote is the fallback per-path remote (the peer's wireguard
355		// endpoint) applied to any path without its own dest_addr. It may be set by
356		// ParseEndpoint BEFORE Open, so it is stored here and applied at Open time.
357		defaultRemote    netip.AddrPort
358		hasDefaultRemote bool
359	
360		// fecCfg / adaptiveCfg are the FEC configuration (T24/T29), shared by every peer; the
361		// per-peer fecSend/fecRecv runtime state Open builds from them lives on peerState. nil
362		// when FEC (respectively adaptive FEC) is disabled — the datapath is then byte-for-byte
363		// the pre-T24 behaviour. See fec.go.
364		fecCfg      *fec.Config
365		adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config
366	
367		// Receive fan-in (T30). To let a path be added at runtime WITHOUT the engine
368		// spawning a new receive goroutine (it only builds its receive goroutines once,
369		// from the ReceiveFuncs Open returns), the per-path socket reads are decoupled
370		// from the engine-facing delivery: the Bind owns one readLoop goroutine PER path
371		// (tracked by readersWG) that reads its socket and feeds the shared resequencer,
372		// and Open returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc that drains the resequencer
373		// in order. A reader pokes deliverSignal after each datagram so the drainer wakes;
374		// recvClosed is closed by Close to release both the drainer and any reader parked
375		// on a delivery. openPort mirrors Open's bind port so a runtime-added path binds
376		// consistently; nextPathID is a monotonic high-water that persists ACROSS Open
377		// spans (a Close->Open never lowers it; only a process restart resets it), so a
378		// surviving path is never renumbered and a freed id is never reused for the
379		// process lifetime, and thus can never collide with the peer's per-path reflector
380		// state.
381		deliverSignal chan struct{}
382		recvClosed    chan struct{}
383		readersWG     sync.WaitGroup
384		openPort      uint16
385		nextPathID    uint16
386	
387		// Receive-path liveness sweep (T39, defect D15). Liveness DOWN-detection normally
388		// rides StartProbeLoop's single wall-clock ticker goroutine (emitProbes → Tick).
389		// Under heavy CPU load — e.g. the concentrator absorbing a saturating forward flood
390		// on 4 vCPU — that timer goroutine can be scheduled with ~1s jitter, delaying a
391		// path-DOWN transition (and thus the reply-direction failover) past the P1 budget.
392		// The per-path receive goroutines, in contrast, are the goroutines the inbound
393		// traffic is ALREADY scheduling, so driving Tick from them re-evaluates liveness
394		// even when the timer goroutine is starved. sweepIntervalNanos is the probe
395		// interval (0 until StartProbeLoop arms it — a bind without the probe transport
396		// never sweeps); lastSweepNanos is the wall-clock high-water throttling the sweep
397		// to at most once per interval so the receive hot path stays cheap.
398		sweepIntervalNanos atomic.Int64
399		lastSweepNanos     atomic.Int64
400	}
401	
402	// compile-time proof that Multipath satisfies the engine's Bind contract.
403	var _ Bind = (*Multipath)(nil)
404	
405	// NewMultipath returns a closed multipath Bind over the configured paths; call
406	// Open to bind the per-path sockets. The PSK keys the outer framing and must be
407	// set (config validation guarantees it). The scheduler is the injected send-side
408	// path-selection policy (T15) whose priority order MUST match the paths slice
409	// order (index 0 = the preferred primary); it is a required collaborator so the
410	// send path is never without a policy.
411	//
412	// probers is the per-path probe initiator that drives on-wire liveness (T13/T37).
413	// When non-nil it MUST hold exactly one *telemetry.Prober per path, in path order,
414	// and those SAME values MUST be the scheduler's PathHealth sources so the liveness
415	// the probe loop measures is the liveness the scheduler selects on. Pass nil to
416	// run the bind without the probe transport (the T12 unit tests drive selection via
417	// sched.AlwaysUp instead). A Reflector is built from the PSK to answer peer probes.
418	//
419	// newProber is the factory the runtime path-add path (AddPath, T30) uses to mint a
420	// prober for a newly-admitted path; it must be non-nil to allow AddPath and MUST be
421	// paired with a non-nil probers (the boot-time set) — a factory without a boot-time
422	// slice would let AddPath append to a nil m.probers, breaking the m.paths/m.probers
423	// alignment invariant and panicking on the next Open at m.probers[i]. Pass nil to
424	// forbid runtime path addition.
425	//
426	// fecCfg is the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon FEC configuration (T24). Pass nil to run the
427	// datapath with FEC disabled (the pre-T24 behaviour, byte-for-byte); a non-nil,
428	// pre-validated config turns the send-side parity encoder and receive-side recovery
429	// decoder on. It is validated here (fail fast) so an invalid ratio is rejected at
430	// construction rather than at the first Open.
431	// adaptiveCfg is the adaptive-FEC controller configuration (T29). Pass nil for the
432	// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24); a non-nil config REQUIRES a non-nil fecCfg (the
433	// controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity — it is meaningless with the plane off)
434	// and reinterprets fecCfg.ParityShards as the controller's parity ceiling. It is
435	// validated here (fail fast) so a mis-tuned control law is rejected at construction.
436	//
437	// amnezia is the tunnel's AmneziaWG obfuscation profile (config.Amnezia). It
438	// parameterizes the send-path frame-type classifier so a WireGuard control frame is
439	// recognised and pacing-exempted under advanced security — custom magic headers and
440	// handshake junk prefixes — as well as in vanilla mode (defect D22). Pass the zero value
441	// for a vanilla (unobfuscated) tunnel; the classifier then uses the default type words
442	// and no junk prefix. It does NOT need to match the config's validated state — an
443	// all-zero profile is exactly the vanilla classifier.
444	func NewMultipath(paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, probers []*telemetry.Prober, newProber ProberFactory, fecCfg *fec.Config, adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config, amnezia config.Amnezia) (*Multipath, error) {
445		if len(paths) == 0 {
446			return nil, errors.New("bind: at least one path is required")
447		}
448		if !psk.IsSet() {
449			return nil, errors.New("bind: PSK is required for outer framing")
450		}
451		if len(paths) > 256 {
452			// path-id is a uint8 in the DATA frame header.
453			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: at most 256 paths supported, got %d", len(paths))
454		}
455		if scheduler == nil {
456			return nil, errors.New("bind: a send scheduler is required")
457		}
458		if newProber != nil && probers == nil {
459			// A runtime-path factory without a boot-time prober slice would let AddPath append
460			// to a nil m.probers, desyncing m.paths from m.probers and panicking on the next
461			// Open at m.probers[i]. The two are paired by construction; enforce it here.
462			return nil, errors.New("bind: newProber requires a non-nil probers (boot-time set)")
463		}
464		if probers != nil && len(probers) != len(paths) {
465			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: probers must have one entry per path (got %d, want %d)", len(probers), len(paths))
466		}
467		for i, pr := range probers {
468			if pr == nil {
469				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: prober %d is nil", i)
470			}
471		}
472		if fecCfg != nil {
473			if err := fecCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
474				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid FEC configuration: %w", err)
475			}
476			if fecCfg.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
477				// A deadline this large makes every deadline-flushed group's recovery
478				// structurally late: the resequencer skips the gap (resequencerTimeout) before
479				// the parity-derived frames land, so recovery never delivers. Reject it rather
480				// than ship a bond whose FEC silently cannot help (defect #4).
481				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: fec deadline %s exceeds the max %s (must stay safely below the resequencer's %s per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped)", fecCfg.Deadline, maxFECDeadline, resequencerTimeout)
482			}
483		}
484		if adaptiveCfg != nil {
485			if fecCfg == nil {
486				return nil, errors.New("bind: adaptive FEC requires a FEC configuration (the controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity)")
487			}
488			if err := adaptiveCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
489				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid adaptive FEC configuration: %w", err)
490			}
491			// The controller's parity ceiling (MaxParity) is the fixed FEC ParityShards both
492			// ends agree on: the receiver's decoder is built at fecCfg.ParityShards, and the
493			// encoder must never emit more parity than that ceiling (SetParity clamps to it).
494			// A mismatch would let the controller target a parity index the decoder rejects,
495			// so bind the two together at construction rather than trusting the caller.
496			if adaptiveCfg.MaxParity != fecCfg.ParityShards {
497				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC parity ceiling (MaxParity=%d) must equal the FEC parity_shards (%d), which the receiver's decoder is built at", adaptiveCfg.MaxParity, fecCfg.ParityShards)
498			}
499			if adaptiveCfg.DataShards != fecCfg.DataShards {
500				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC DataShards (%d) must equal the FEC data_shards (%d)", adaptiveCfg.DataShards, fecCfg.DataShards)
501			}
502		}
503		// The single-peer edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (the primary). virt is
504		// created here — NOT per Open — because the concentrator pins its destination once for
505		// the process life and every existing test relies on the virtual-endpoint pointer being
506		// stable across Open/Close/add/remove.
507		primary := newPeerState("", psk, scheduler, newProber, probers)
508		m := &Multipath{
509			defs:           append([]config.Path(nil), paths...),
510			classify:       newWGClassifier(amnezia),
511			deferredListen: defaultDeferredListen,
512			peerState:      primary,
513			peers:          []*peerState{primary},
514			peersByName:    map[string]*peerState{primary.name: primary},
515			peerByEndpoint: map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState{},
516			peerBySource:   map[netip.Addr]*peerState{},
517			peerByVirt:     map[*udpEndpoint]*peerState{primary.virt: primary},
518			fecCfg:         fecCfg,
519			adaptiveCfg:    adaptiveCfg,
520		}
521		// Publish the initial (primary-only) peer view the receive drainer iterates. No
522		// concurrency yet — the bind is not open — so this runs without m.mu.
523		m.republishPeersLocked()
524		return m, nil
525	}
526	
527	// republishPeersLocked snapshots m.peers into the lock-free peersView the engine-facing
528	// receive drainer (newReceiveFunc) iterates. The caller MUST hold m.mu whenever the bind
529	// can be open (so the snapshot never races a concurrent m.peers append); the constructor
530	// is the sole exception (it runs before the Multipath is reachable by any goroutine). The
531	// snapshot is a fresh slice so a later append to m.peers never mutates a view the drainer
532	// is mid-iteration over.
533	func (m *Multipath) republishPeersLocked() {
534		snap := make([]*peerState, len(m.peers))
535		copy(snap, m.peers)
536		m.peersView.Store(&snap)
537	}
538	
539	// Open binds one UDP socket per configured path to that path's source address on
540	// port (0 = random per socket), sets a large SO_RCVBUF on each, spawns one
541	// Bind-owned reader per path, and returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc (the
542	// fan-in drainer) plus the first path's bound port. Each path whose config carries
543	// a dest_addr — or, failing that, the peer's wireguard endpoint learned via
544	// ParseEndpoint — starts with a known remote; the rest are learned from inbound
545	// traffic.
546	//
547	// Open is the sole creator of the per-path sockets: the engine's bring-up path
548	// calls Close() first (on the still-unopened bind) and then Open(), so building
549	// the sockets HERE — not in NewMultipath — is what makes that Close→Open sequence
550	// (and every subsequent Down→Up) work. The engine passes the previously-bound
551	// port back on a re-Open; each path binds to it on its own distinct source
552	// address, and the first path's bound port is returned so the engine keeps a
553	// stable listen port across the cycle (matching conn.StdNetBind).
554	func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
555		m.mu.Lock()
556		defer m.mu.Unlock()
557		if len(m.paths) != 0 {
558			return nil, 0, conn.ErrBindAlreadyOpen
559		}
560	
561		sendCodec, err := m.newCodec()
562		if err != nil {
563			return nil, 0, err
564		}
565	
566		// A fresh resequencer per Open: its release point re-pins to the first frame
567		// received after this bring-up, so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect) never
568		// wedges on a stale high-water outer-seq.
569		m.resequencer.Store(reseq.New(resequencerWindow, resequencerTimeout, reseq.SystemClock{}))
570	
571		// Fresh FEC send/receive state per Open, when FEC is enabled (T24). The encoder
572		// group state and the decoder's per-group buffers re-pin with the sockets, so a
573		// Close→Open cycle never reconstructs against a stale group. Both are torn down in
574		// closeSocketsLocked. A build error here is a programmer error (the ratio was
575		// validated in NewMultipath), so it fails the Open.
576		if m.fecCfg != nil {
577			enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(*m.fecCfg, fec.SystemClock{})
578			if err != nil {
579				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
580				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC encoder: %w", err)
581			}
582			dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(*m.fecCfg)
583			if err != nil {
584				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
585				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC decoder: %w", err)
586			}
587			dec.SetRetainWindow(fecRetainGroups)
588			// Quiescence-accurate unrecoverable accounting (D24): fold a doomed group into
589			// the unrecoverable counter once its recovery could no longer have helped, even
590			// when no newer group arrives to advance the retain-window eviction (a stalled
591			// link, or traffic that simply stops after an incident). Past fecRecoveryDeadline
592			// from a group's first-seen instant, its deadline-flushed parity has certainly
593			// arrived (encoder grouping deadline) AND the resequencer has certainly skipped
594			// its gap (resequencerTimeout), so any later reconstruction is delivered too late
595			// to matter — the group is definitively unrecoverable and safe to count.
596			dec.SetClock(fec.SystemClock{})
597			dec.SetRecoveryDeadline(m.fecCfg.Deadline + resequencerTimeout)
598			m.fecSend = &fecSender{enc: enc}
599			// Adaptive mode (T29): a fresh controller per Open, re-pinned with the encoder so a
600			// Close→Open cycle starts the control law from M=0 (no standing redundancy until
601			// loss is observed) rather than inheriting a stale target. The controller cfg was
602			// validated in NewMultipath, so a build error here is a programmer error and fails
603			// the Open. The receive-side residual-loss estimator (post-FEC-recovery loss, the
604			// P4 signal) is likewise fresh per span.
605			if m.adaptiveCfg != nil {
606				ctrl, err := adaptivefec.NewController(*m.adaptiveCfg, adaptivefec.SystemClock{})
607				if err != nil {
608					_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
609					return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: build adaptive FEC controller: %w", err)
610				}
611				m.fecSend.ctrl = ctrl
612				// Adopt the controller's starting parity (0 — no standing redundancy until loss
613				// is observed) so the encoder and controller agree from t=0; the tick loop then
614				// sizes it to measured loss within the first control interval. Fixed mode leaves
615				// the encoder at its cfg.ParityShards default instead.
616				enc.SetParity(ctrl.Parity())
617			}
618			m.fecRecv.Store(&fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)})
619		}
620	
621		// Resolve every path's interface up front and decide, per path, whether its
622		// socket may be device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard — survives a T16
623		// re-roam) or must pin the specific source IP (the pre-T16 behaviour, required
624		// when paths share an interface or the interface is multi-address, so distinct
625		// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port without an EADDRINUSE collision). See
626		// selectDeviceBinds.
627		srcs := make([]netip.Addr, len(m.defs))
628		for i := range m.defs {
629			srcs[i] = m.defs[i].SourceAddr
630		}
631		bindDevs := planPathBinds(srcs)
632	
633		// A path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr is merely NOT-YET-ASSIGNABLE at boot
634		// (EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the address — a 5G modem without a DHCP
635		// lease, Starlink mid-obstruction) is DEFERRED rather than treated as fatal, so
636		// the bond comes up on the paths that DO bind (G2/W1, approach A). Tolerating it
637		// requires the runtime path-down machinery: the deferred path's prober stays
638		// StateDown so the scheduler excludes it, and a DynamicScheduler whose membership
639		// can be reconciled to only the bound paths. Absent the probe transport (the T12
640		// no-prober unit binds) or a dynamic scheduler there is no such Down model, so —
641		// exactly as AddPath refuses a runtime add without probers — every bind error
642		// stays fatal there. A MALFORMED source_addr never reaches here (config.validate
643		// rejects it at load), and any OTHER bind error (EADDRINUSE, permission) is fatal.
644		dyn, dynOK := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
645		tolerateDefer := m.probers != nil && dynOK
646		m.deferred = nil
647	
648		actualPort := port
649		firstBound := true
650		// boundProbers is index-aligned with m.paths (the paths that actually bound), so
651		// the scheduler reconcile below and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping stay aligned
652		// even when a middle path deferred. nil in the no-prober case (nothing to reconcile).
653		var boundProbers []*telemetry.Prober
654		for i := range m.defs {
655			def := m.defs[i]
656			// Device-bind this path when selectDeviceBinds proved it safe (so a
657			// mid-session source-address change / T16 re-roam does not break the socket),
658			// otherwise pin the specific source IP. See selectDeviceBinds / listenPath.
659			c, err := listenPath(def.SourceAddr, port, bindDevs[i])
660			if err != nil {
661				if tolerateDefer && errors.Is(err, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL) {
662					// Defer this path: record its def + boot prober (kept Down) for the T55
663					// background reconcile to retry, and leave the bond to come up on the rest.
664					// Mirrors AddPath's rollback discipline — a failed path never disturbs the
665					// tunnel — at the boot boundary.
666					m.deferred = append(m.deferred, deferredPath{def: def, prober: m.probers[i]})
667					continue
668				}
669				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
670				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: open path %q on %s: %w", def.Name, def.SourceAddr, err)
671			}
672			// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort: the kernel caps at net.core.rmem_max and
673			// SetReadBuffer does not require privilege, so a returned error is rare;
674			// treat it as non-fatal rather than refusing to bind in a restricted env.
675			_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
676	
677			// The path binds to the primary peer here; its receive codec is that peer's codec
678			// (reached through the embedded primary, as the datapath reaches m.sendCodec).
679			codec, err := m.newCodec()
680			if err != nil {
681				_ = c.Close()
682				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
683				return nil, 0, err
684			}
685	
686			shared := &sharedPathState{name: def.Name, id: uint8(i), src: def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
687			ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec}
688			if m.probers != nil {
689				ps.prober = m.probers[i]
690				// Reconcile the DATA-frame path-id to the prober's IMMUTABLE stamp rather than
691				// to the slice index i: after a runtime RemovePath the survivor keeps its
692				// original (higher) stamp, so index-based numbering would renumber a live path
693				// AND diverge its DATA id from its PROBE stamp. Taking id from the prober keeps
694				// DATA and PROBE agreeing on the wire and a survivor's id stable across a reopen.
695				shared.id = ps.prober.PathID()
696				boundProbers = append(boundProbers, ps.prober)
697			}
698			switch {
699			case def.DestAddr.IsValid():
700				ps.setRemote(def.DestAddr)
701			case m.hasDefaultRemote:
702				ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
703			}
704			m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
705			m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
706			if firstBound {
707				actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
708				firstBound = false
709			}
710		}
711	
712		// Hard guard (a): a bond with NO transport is impossible. If every configured path
713		// deferred, tolerance must NOT degrade to a zero-path bind — fail fatally, exactly
714		// as the pre-tolerance Open did when the sole path could not bind.
715		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
716			_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
717			return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: no configured path could bind its source address (all %d deferred as not-yet-assignable)", len(m.deferred))
718		}
719	
720		// nextPathID is a MONOTONIC HIGH-WATER carried ACROSS Open spans, never decreased.
721		// Close does not reset it, and here it is only ever RAISED to at least max(existing
722		// path stamp)+1. Resetting it to len(m.paths) (the pre-fix behaviour) re-minted an id
723		// a survivor still held once a RemovePath had opened a gap in the stamp space below
724		// that survivor's stamp: the next runtime AddPath then collided with the live path at
725		// an identical (PathID, SessionID), and because the peer's Reflector keys anti-replay
726		// AND the session challenge PER PathID, the strict-monotonic replay filter dropped one
727		// of the two independent ProbeSeq streams -> probe loss / false-DOWN. Deriving the
728		// high-water from the (prober-stamped) ps.id covers both the probe-transport case and
729		// the T12 no-prober case (where ps.id == i, so the high-water is len(m.paths)).
730		m.openPort = port
731		for _, ps := range m.paths {
732			if uint16(ps.id)+1 > m.nextPathID {
733				m.nextPathID = uint16(ps.id) + 1
734			}
735		}
736		// A DEFERRED path's boot prober already OWNS its path-id stamp; raise the
737		// high-water past it too, so a runtime AddPath arriving while the path is still
738		// deferred cannot mint that reserved id and collide with it once T55 binds it.
739		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
740			if dp.prober != nil {
741				if uint16(dp.prober.PathID())+1 > m.nextPathID {
742					m.nextPathID = uint16(dp.prober.PathID()) + 1
743				}
744			}
745		}
746		m.sendCodec = sendCodec
747	
748		// Reconcile the scheduler's membership with the path slice just rebuilt from
749		// m.defs. A runtime AddPath/RemovePath (T30) keeps m.defs, m.probers, AND the
750		// live scheduler in lockstep during an Open span; re-pinning the scheduler's
751		// health list to the BOUND probers HERE — index-aligned with m.paths, since each
752		// bound path appended its ps.prober to boundProbers in order above — is the single
753		// reconciliation point that makes that runtime membership survive this
754		// Close→Open cycle without a scheduler/path desync (no frozen zombie health
755		// entry, no resurrected removed path). A DEFERRED path (not-yet-assignable
756		// source_addr) is deliberately EXCLUDED here: its prober is absent from the
757		// scheduler, so Pick can never select it, and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping
758		// stays index-aligned with the bound-only path slice. Only meaningful with the
759		// probe transport: a bind without probers cannot change membership at runtime
760		// (AddPath is refused) and never defers, so its scheduler is left exactly as built.
761		if m.probers != nil && dynOK {
762			health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(boundProbers))
763			for i, pr := range boundProbers {
764				health[i] = pr
765			}
766			if err := dyn.SetPaths(health); err != nil {
767				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
768				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: reconcile scheduler on open: %w", err)
769			}
770		}
771	
772		// Stand up the receive fan-in: one Bind-owned reader per path feeding the shared
773		// resequencer, and a single engine-facing drainer. Both channels are recreated
774		// per Open so a Close→Open cycle starts clean.
775		m.deliverSignal = make(chan struct{}, 1)
776		m.recvClosed = make(chan struct{})
777		for _, ps := range m.paths {
778			m.readersWG.Add(1)
779			go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)
780		}
781		// Deadline-tick goroutine for FEC group close (T24): it flushes a partial group's
782		// parity on time under low load so a group is never stranded waiting for the size
783		// threshold. Tracked by readersWG (like the readers) so Close waits for it; it
784		// exits on recvClosed. It TryLocks m.mu, so it never blocks Close's readersWG.Wait
785		// held under m.mu.
786		if m.fecSend != nil {
787			m.readersWG.Add(1)
788			go m.fecTickLoop(m.fecCfg.Deadline, m.recvClosed)
789		}
790		return []ReceiveFunc{m.newReceiveFunc(m.deliverSignal, m.recvClosed)}, actualPort, nil
791	}
792	
793	// readLoop is one Bind-OWNED per-path receive goroutine (T30). It reads the path
794	// socket and dispatches every datagram through handleInbound — which pushes DATA
795	// into the shared resequencer and answers/consumes PROBEs — then pokes the delivery
796	// signal so the single engine-facing drainer wakes to release any newly in-order
797	// frame. The read buffer is private to this goroutine, so the per-path Codec's
798	// scratch is never shared.
799	//
800	// Owning the readers in the Bind (rather than returning one ReceiveFunc per path to
801	// the engine, as T12 did) is what makes a runtime path add/remove possible without
802	// disturbing the engine: the engine builds its receive goroutines ONCE from Open's
803	// return, so a path added later gets a reader HERE while the engine's fixed receive
804	// set — and thus the WG session — sees no churn. The goroutine exits when its socket
805	// is closed (RemovePath drains one path; Close drains all).
806	func (m *Multipath) readLoop(ps *peerPathState, deliver chan<- struct{}) {
807		defer m.readersWG.Done()
808		readBuf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
809		for {
810			n, srcAP, err := ps.conn.ReadFromUDPAddrPort(readBuf)
811			if err != nil {
812				return // socket closed: this path was removed, or the bind was closed
813			}
814			// Per-path received-wire accounting (T23): count the OUTER datagram this path
815			// pulled off its socket before dispatch, lock-free. This goroutine is the sole
816			// writer of ps.rxBytes, so the atomic Add is uncontended.
817			ps.rxBytes.Add(uint64(n))
818			m.handleInbound(ps, readBuf[:n], srcAP)
819			// Advance liveness off the receive path (throttled): a live signal on THIS path
820			// is what lets a DIFFERENT, silent path be marked DOWN promptly even when the
821			// probe-loop ticker is starved under load (D15). The peer probes every path
822			// (and floods the survivor after it roams), so the surviving path's reader
823			// supplies exactly that signal.
824			m.tickLivenessFromReceive(time.Now())
825			// Non-blocking poke: a single buffered slot coalesces bursts; the drainer
826			// re-checks the resequencer on every wake, so a coalesced poke loses nothing.
827			select {
828			case deliver <- struct{}{}:
829			default:
830			}
831		}
832	}
833	
834	// tickLivenessFromReceive re-evaluates EVERY path's liveness from a receive
835	// goroutine, throttled to at most once per probe interval (T39, defect D15). It is
836	// the starvation-robust companion to StartProbeLoop's timer-driven Tick: the timer
837	// goroutine can be delayed ~1s under CPU saturation, but the receive goroutines are
838	// scheduled by the very traffic that must trigger failover, so liveness advances
839	// regardless. Ticking is monotone-safe: Tick only marks an UP path DOWN once its
840	// silence STRICTLY exceeds DownAfter and never brings a path UP (that needs
841	// RecordEcho), so a more frequent Tick can only make a genuine DOWN transition land
842	// sooner — never a premature/false one — and the failback hysteresis (owned by the
843	// scheduler) is untouched. No-op when the probe transport is absent (interval unset).
844	func (m *Multipath) tickLivenessFromReceive(now time.Time) {
845		interval := m.sweepIntervalNanos.Load()
846		if interval == 0 {
847			return // no probe transport / loop not started: nothing to Tick
848		}
849		n := now.UnixNano()
850		last := m.lastSweepNanos.Load()
851		if n-last < interval {
852			return // throttled: this interval's sweep was already taken
853		}
854		if !m.lastSweepNanos.CompareAndSwap(last, n) {
855			return // another receive goroutine won this interval's sweep
856		}
857		// Snapshot the prober set under m.mu (a runtime AddPath/RemovePath mutates it),
858		// then Tick OUTSIDE the lock so a transition's log write never runs under m.mu.
859		// TryLock, NOT Lock: Close holds m.mu WHILE it waits on readersWG for the readers to
860		// exit, so a reader that BLOCKED here on m.mu would deadlock that shutdown. The sweep
861		// is opportunistic and throttled, so when the lock is contended (a concurrent
862		// Close/AddPath/RemovePath/Send) simply skipping this interval is harmless — the
863		// probe-loop ticker and the next receive still advance liveness. This preserves
864		// Close's invariant that a reader never blocks on m.mu. The lock, when taken, is held
865		// at most once per interval (~5/s) for a bounded snapshot, so it adds negligible
866		// contention to Send and does not disturb the lock-free receive fast path.
867		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
868			return
869		}
870		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, 0, len(m.paths))
871		for _, ps := range m.paths {
872			if ps.prober != nil {
873				probers = append(probers, ps.prober)
874			}
875		}
876		m.mu.Unlock()
877		for _, pr := range probers {
878			pr.Tick()
879		}
880		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18, the repeated-flap wedge). The active-backup
881		// selection is otherwise recomputed ONLY inside the scheduler's Pick, which the
882		// Bind calls ONLY from Send — so failover is pull-based: it happens only when the
883		// engine hands down an egress datagram. When the ACTIVE path dies during an egress
884		// LULL that is fatal: a repeated-flap kill landing on the just-restored primary
885		// before the saturating flow re-fills it (the failback and the next kill overlap)
886		// leaves both TCP directions stalled on the now-dead path, so NO Send occurs, so
887		// Pick is never called, so egress is never switched to the healthy backup — the
888		// bond wedges on the dead path until the 25s WG keepalive finally drives a Send.
889		// The receive tick is the starvation-robust signal that ALREADY detected the DOWN
890		// (it just Ticked the path down above); recomputing the scheduler from it makes the
891		// switch EAGER — bounded by the detection window (~DownAfter + one interval) rather
892		// than by the next application Send. Pick recomputes purely against current liveness
893		// and the clock and the failback dwell is time-based, so a more frequent recompute
894		// can only make a genuine transition land sooner — never a premature/false failover,
895		// and never a shortened anti-thrash hysteresis. It takes ONLY the scheduler's own
896		// lock (never m.mu), so it adds no receive-path m.mu contention and cannot invert
897		// the Send-path m.mu→scheduler lock order.
898		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
899	}
900	
901	// nudgeSchedulerActive forces the scheduler to recompute its active egress path
902	// against current liveness, independent of any application Send. It is the eager-
903	// failover companion to the Send-driven Pick: driven from the liveness-detection
904	// paths (the receive tick and the probe loop) it switches egress to a healthy backup
905	// the moment the active path is detected DOWN, so a dead active path with stalled
906	// egress does not wedge the bond until the next Send (defect D18). The returned index
907	// is intentionally discarded — only the recompute (and its logged transition) is
908	// wanted here; Send remains the sole reader of the selection for actual routing. Pick
909	// is internally synchronized and never calls back into the Bind, so this is safe to
910	// call from a receive goroutine or the probe loop without m.mu.
911	//
912	// It calls Recompute, NOT Pick: the nudge wants only the liveness-driven active-set
913	// recompute (and its logged transition), and a weighted/aggregating scheduler's Pick
914	// is STATEFUL — a spurious Pick here would consume a distribution slot and skew the
915	// per-path weight split. Recompute is the non-consuming half that refreshes the
916	// eligible/active set without touching distribution/pacing/load state, so the T40
917	// eager-failover guarantee holds for BOTH the active-backup and the weighted policy
918	// (defect D18).
919	func (m *Multipath) nudgeSchedulerActive() {
920		m.scheduler.Recompute()
921	}
922	
923	// newReceiveFunc returns the SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc: it drains EACH bound peer's
924	// resequencer in that peer's own outer-seq order and hands each inner datagram up stamped
925	// with THAT peer's stable virtual endpoint (per-packet endpoint fill), so the engine
926	// attributes return traffic to the right peer and Send routes replies back via that peer's
927	// virt (invariant A1: one virtual endpoint per peer). A path's reader (handleInbound) has
928	// already routed each DATA frame to its OWNING peer's resequencer via the peerPathState's
929	// ps.peer back-reference, so a shared socket serving many peers keeps each peer's stream
930	// isolated; this drainer just fans the in-order releases back in. Because every path's
931	// reader feeds one of these resequencers and a single drainer releases them, a path (or
932	// peer) added or removed at runtime needs no change to the engine's receive set.
933	//
934	// Fairness: the peers are scanned round-robin from a rotating cursor so a saturated peer
935	// cannot starve another peer's in-order releases (single-peer edge/hub: the cursor is a
936	// no-op). When nothing is ready across ANY peer it parks until a reader pokes deliver,
937	// until Close closes closed, or until a short poll elapses — the poll guarantees a
938	// head-of-line-blocked run still makes timeout progress even if the last live path fell
939	// silent right after buffering it. A single drainer delivers with ZERO added reorder
940	// (only it calls Pop), which is stricter than T12's per-path receivers.
941	func (m *Multipath) newReceiveFunc(deliver <-chan struct{}, closed <-chan struct{}) ReceiveFunc {
942		// One reusable poll timer per drainer, NOT a fresh time.After timer per park: the
943		// drainer parks on every empty receive, and a per-park 250 ms timer — retained by
944		// the runtime timer heap until it fires even after the park is over — added
945		// avoidable allocation and GC pressure on the receive hot path (opus low defect).
946		// The ReceiveFunc is driven by a single engine goroutine, so the timer has no
947		// concurrent user; Stop+drain then Reset per park reuses the one allocation.
948		timer := time.NewTimer(resequencerTimeout)
949		stopTimer := func() {
950			timer.Stop()
951			select {
952			case <-timer.C:
953			default:
954			}
955		}
956		stopTimer() // start inert; armed only while parked
957		// rr is the round-robin cursor, advanced past a peer each time it yields a frame so
958		// the next receive starts at the following peer. It is touched only by this single
959		// engine goroutine, so it needs no synchronisation.
960		var rr int
961		return func(packets [][]byte, sizes []int, eps []Endpoint) (int, error) {
962			for {
963				// Scan every bound peer round-robin for an in-order resequenced DATA frame.
964				// The item carries the outer source of the frame that produced it, so the
965				// peer's virtual endpoint pins correctly even when the frame was buffered and
966				// released out of arrival order. The peer view is read lock-free (peersView),
967				// so a concurrent peer wiring/fan-out never contends m.mu here.
968				peers := *m.peersView.Load()
969				n := len(peers)
970				progressed := false
971				for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
972					ps := peers[(rr+i)%n]
973					rq := ps.resequencer.Load()
974					if rq == nil {
975						continue // a peer not yet Open on this span has no resequencer
976					}
977					it, ok := rq.Pop()
978					if !ok {
979						continue
980					}
981					rr = (rr + i + 1) % n // start the next scan after this peer (fairness)
982					if len(it.Payload) > len(packets[0]) {
983						// Oversize inner datagram: drop it, but a frame WAS dequeued this pass,
984						// so keep draining (re-scan) rather than parking.
985						progressed = true
986						break
987					}
988					sizes[0] = copy(packets[0], it.Payload)
989					eps[0] = m.virtualEndpoint(ps, it.Src)
990					return 1, nil
991				}
992				if progressed {
993					continue // dropped an oversize frame; re-scan before parking
994				}
995				timer.Reset(resequencerTimeout)
996				select {
997				case <-deliver:
998					stopTimer()
999				case <-timer.C:
1000					// Poll fired; its channel is already drained by this receive.
1001				case <-closed:
1002					stopTimer()
1003					return 0, errClosed
1004				}
1005			}
1006		}
1007	}
1008	
1009	// handleInbound decodes one received outer datagram and dispatches it by kind. It
1010	// is the single per-frame receive action, factored out of readLoop so the probe
1011	// transport is exercisable without spinning receive goroutines. Delivery up the WG
1012	// path is deferred to the resequencer (Pop, in the engine-facing drainer): a DATA
1013	// frame is not handed up here but pushed into the shared resequencer to be released in
1014	// outer-seq order.
1015	//
1016	//   - DATA: the decoded inner datagram is pushed into the resequencer keyed by
1017	//     the frame's outer-seq (delivered later, in order, via Pop). The path's
1018	//     remote is NOT learned here — remote-learning is authenticated-only (see
1019	//     below), so a forged DATA frame cannot steer a path's return endpoint.
1020	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=false: an authenticated peer probe. Its source is learned as
1021	//     the path's remote (D11: a probe-only backup path gets a usable return remote
1022	//     before any DATA flows) and it is reflected straight back to that source via
1023	//     this path's socket (T13 Reflector). Reflection writes independently of the
1024	//     scheduler/getRemote so an echo returns even on a not-yet-active path.
1025	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=true: an authenticated echo of one of our own probes. Its
1026	//     source is learned as the remote too, and the raw echo is fed into this
1027	//     path's Prober (HandleEcho) to update RTT/loss and drive liveness.
1028	//   - anything else (PARITY/CONTROL/malformed): dropped.
1029	//
1030	// Remote-learning and reflection touch only authenticated (MAC-verified) PROBE
1031	// frames — Decode has already verified the tag for the PROBE kind — which is what
