1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"net"
6		"net/netip"
7		"sync"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
11	)
12	
13	// DefaultReconcileInterval is the cadence at which StartReconcileLoop re-attempts the
14	// bind of the paths a tolerant Open (T51) left DEFERRED. It is a seconds-scale poll,
15	// not the sub-second probe cadence: a deferred path is a boot-time transient (a 5G
16	// modem acquiring a DHCP lease, Starlink emerging from an obstruction), an event that
17	// resolves on a human/network timescale, so a 1s cadence bounds the promote latency
18	// tightly while the tick — a single mutex-guarded length check when nothing is
19	// deferred (the steady state) — stays negligible.
20	const DefaultReconcileInterval = 1 * time.Second
21	
22	// defaultDeferredListen is the production bind for a reconciled deferred path: it pins
23	// the path's source IP on the bind's shared port, identical to AddPath's runtime bind
24	// (net.ListenUDP on the source). It returns EADDRNOTAVAIL for as long as no interface
25	// holds the address — which is exactly the signal reconcileDeferred reads to keep the
26	// path deferred and retry — and succeeds once the address becomes assignable.
27	func defaultDeferredListen(src netip.Addr, port uint16) (*net.UDPConn, error) {
28		laddr := &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.IP(src.AsSlice()), Port: int(port)}
29		return net.ListenUDP("udp", laddr)
30	}
31	
32	// StartReconcileLoop launches the background deferred-path reconciler (T55): every
33	// interval it re-attempts the bind of the paths a tolerant Open (T51) left DEFERRED
34	// (a well-formed source_addr that was not yet assignable at boot) and PROMOTES any
35	// that now binds to a live path — into m.paths, the scheduler, and its own reader —
36	// so a source_addr that becomes assignable after boot (a 5G modem that finally got
37	// its DHCP lease) is brought into the running bond WITHOUT a restart. It stops when
38	// the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent), so the daemon shuts it down before
39	// Close with no goroutine leak — the symmetric companion to StartProbeLoop. It is a
40	// no-op (returning a no-op stopper) on a bind without the probe transport (which never
41	// defers — Open makes every bind error fatal there) or interval <= 0.
42	//
43	// Mechanism (design decision): a bounded periodic POLL, not netlink route/addr
44	// subscription. Event-driven netlink (vishvananda/netlink AddrSubscribe) would add a
45	// new external dependency this module does not carry, and the deferred set is normally
46	// EMPTY (every path bound at boot), so the poll degenerates to a cheap length check;
47	// when a path is deferred, re-attempting a non-blocking ListenUDP once per interval
48	// promotes it within a bounded window. A fixed interval rather than a backoff: the
49	// deferred window is a bounded boot-time transient, so a steady cadence bounds the
50	// promote latency without a backoff schedule's complexity.
51	//
52	// The loop owns its own done channel (like StartProbeLoop) rather than riding the
53	// per-Open recvClosed: it is a DEVICE-lifecycle goroutine started after the first Open
54	// and stopped before Close, and it operates on whatever m.deferred the current Open
55	// span holds (a Close→Open rebuilds m.deferred; reconcileDeferred is a no-op while the
56	// bind is closed). Its cadence uses a wall-clock ticker because it is production timing
57	// glue; the bind mutation it performs is exercised directly in tests against the
58	// injected listen seam, so a test never starts this goroutine.
59	func (m *Multipath) StartReconcileLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
60		if m.newProber == nil || interval <= 0 {
61			return func() {}
62		}
63		done := make(chan struct{})
64		go func() {
65			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
66			defer ticker.Stop()
67			for {
68				select {
69				case <-done:
70					return
71				case <-ticker.C:
72					m.reconcileDeferred()
73				}
74			}
75		}()
76		var once sync.Once
77		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
78	}
79	
80	// reconcileDeferred is one background reconcile step (T55): it re-attempts the bind of
81	// every DEFERRED path and promotes any that now binds to a live path, leaving the rest
82	// deferred for the next tick. A path that still cannot bind — EADDRNOTAVAIL (address
83	// still not assignable) OR any other transient bind error — stays deferred and is
84	// retried; a bind fault never becomes fatal to the RUNNING bond (the tunnel is already
85	// up on the paths that bound). It runs entirely under m.mu, so it serializes with
86	// Send/Close/AddPath/RemovePath and the path slice + scheduler mutate together, and it
87	// is a no-op on a CLOSED bind (len(m.paths)==0) — so it races a concurrent Close
88	// harmlessly (Close either ran first, and this no-ops, or runs after and joins the
89	// reader this step just spawned via readersWG) — or when nothing is deferred (the
90	// steady state, a single length check).
91	func (m *Multipath) reconcileDeferred() {
92		m.mu.Lock()
93		defer m.mu.Unlock()
94		if len(m.paths) == 0 || len(m.deferred) == 0 {
95			return // closed, or nothing to promote
96		}
97		// A deferred path exists only under the runtime path-down model tolerance requires
98		// (probe transport + DynamicScheduler); Open never defers without it, so this
99		// assertion holds. Guard defensively rather than type-assert-panic on the hot lock.
100		dyn, ok := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
101		if !ok {
102			return
103		}
104		// In-place filter: keep the paths that did NOT promote this tick. kept aliases the
105		// deferred backing array, and its length never exceeds the loop index, so appending
106		// a still-deferred entry never clobbers one not yet read (the standard filter idiom).
107		kept := m.deferred[:0]
108		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
109			c, err := m.deferredListen(dp.def.SourceAddr, m.openPort)
110			if err != nil {
111				kept = append(kept, dp) // still not assignable (or a transient fault): retry next tick
112				continue
113			}
114			if err := m.promoteDeferredLocked(dyn, dp, c); err != nil {
115				// The bind succeeded but promotion did not (a scheduler/path index skew, or a
116				// codec build error): close the fresh socket and keep the path deferred so the
117				// next tick retries cleanly, rather than leaking the socket or half-admitting.
118				_ = c.Close()
119				kept = append(kept, dp)
120			}
121		}
122		m.deferred = kept
123	}
124	
125	// promoteDeferredLocked turns a just-bound deferred path into a LIVE one under m.mu. It
126	// wraps the fresh socket in a pathState that REUSES the deferred path's preserved boot
127	// prober — so the promoted path keeps its reserved path-id STAMP (no renumber, no
128	// collision with the peer's per-path reflector state), the continuity T51 preserved for
129	// exactly this step — seeds its remote from the config dest_addr or the learned default,
130	// appends it to m.paths, admits its prober to the scheduler as the new lowest-priority
131	// path, and spawns its Bind-owned reader. It mirrors AddPath's admission MINUS minting a
132	// prober and MINUS touching the durable membership (a deferred path is ALREADY in
133	// m.defs/m.probers and its id-stamp is ALREADY reserved in m.nextPathID by Open), so the
134	// caller need only drop it from m.deferred on success. It rolls a failed admission back
135	// completely and returns the error, leaving m.paths and the scheduler as it found them.
136	func (m *Multipath) promoteDeferredLocked(dyn sched.DynamicScheduler, dp deferredPath, c *net.UDPConn) error {
137		// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort (kernel-capped, needs no privilege) — as in Open/AddPath.
138		_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
139		// The deferred-path machinery is single-peer today, so this attaches the primary peer's
140		// view; its receive codec is that peer's codec, reached through the embedded primary.
141		codec, err := m.newCodec()
142		if err != nil {
143			return err // programmer error (psk validated at construction); never hit in practice
144		}
145		// Reuse the boot prober's IMMUTABLE stamp for the DATA-frame path-id, exactly as Open
146		// and AddPath do, so DATA and PROBE agree on the wire and the promoted path is never
147		// renumbered. The deferred-path machinery is single-peer today (the concentrator fan-out
148		// of a promoted deferred path is a later G4 task), so this attaches the primary peer's
149		// view over the freshly-bound shared socket.
150		shared := &sharedPathState{name: dp.def.Name, id: dp.prober.PathID(), src: dp.def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
151		ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec, prober: dp.prober}
152		switch {
153		case dp.def.DestAddr.IsValid():
154			ps.setRemote(dp.def.DestAddr)
155		case m.hasDefaultRemote:
156			ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
157		}
158		// Append to the shared socket list + the peer's path slice, then admit the prober to the
159		// scheduler as the new tail; the peer's path slice and its scheduler are index-aligned,
160		// so the scheduler's returned index must equal the new path's slice index. A mismatch
161		// would mis-route datagrams, so fail loudly and roll back.
162		m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
163		m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
164		schedIdx, err := dyn.AddPath(ps.prober)
165		if err != nil {
166			m.paths = m.paths[:len(m.paths)-1]
167			m.shared = m.shared[:len(m.shared)-1]
168			return err
169		}
170		if schedIdx != len(m.paths)-1 {
171			bindIdx := len(m.paths) - 1
172			_ = dyn.RemovePath(schedIdx)
173			m.paths = m.paths[:len(m.paths)-1]
174			m.shared = m.shared[:len(m.shared)-1]
175			return fmt.Errorf("bind: scheduler/path index skew after deferred promote: sched=%d bind=%d", schedIdx, bindIdx)
176		}
177		// Spawn the promoted path's Bind-owned reader on the CURRENT Open span's delivery
178		// signal; readersWG tracks it so a subsequent Close joins it (no goroutine leak).
179		m.readersWG.Add(1)
180		go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)
181		return nil
182	}

1	package reseq
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injected time source. Production wires a monotonic wall clock
10	// (SystemClock); tests wire a hand-advanced fake so ordering, window, and
11	// timeout behaviour are deterministic with no real sleeps. It is intentionally
12	// structurally identical to telemetry.Clock so the same fake can drive both.
13	type Clock interface {
14		Now() time.Time
15	}
16	
17	// SystemClock is the production Clock backed by the monotonic wall clock.
18	type SystemClock struct{}
19	
20	// Now returns the current monotonic time.
21	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
22	
23	// Discontinuity/resync guard tuning. A DATA frame is UNAUTHENTICATED by design
24	// (internal/frame/frame.go: "DATA/PARITY forgeable by design"), so a garbage
25	// datagram decoding as KindData (p ~ 1/256) delivers a uniformly-random uint64
26	// OuterSeq, and a genuine peer PROCESS RESTART resets the peer's outerSeq counter
27	// to 1. Neither may be trusted to move the release point arbitrarily.
28	const (
29		// resyncFactor (K) bounds how far AHEAD of the release point a single
30		// unauthenticated frame is trusted to advance the window as a plausible loss
31		// burst. Under bounded cross-path reorder a legitimate frame sits within one
32		// window of next; a forward jump up to K windows is still treated as a loss
33		// burst (the sound bounded-memory window-advance handles it in O(window)), but
34		// a jump of K windows or more is SUSPECT: a single such frame never advances
35		// next. K=4 leaves generous headroom above the 1-window reorder bound while
36		// capping the release point a single trusted frame can skip to (K-1) windows.
37		resyncFactor = 4
38	
39		// resyncCorroborate (C) is how many out-of-band frames carrying DISTINCT seqs
40		// that mutually span less than one window must be observed before the release
41		// point is re-pinned. A genuine peer restart (1,2,3,...) or a consistent
42		// long-outage forward jump emits connected, distinct seqs that trivially
43		// corroborate. A repeated identical seq does NOT advance the count: a single
44		// junk or forged datagram re-delivered — a network duplicate, or a forger
45		// replaying one datagram — contributes only ONE distinct seq, so it can never
46		// self-corroborate. Corroboration therefore requires C INDEPENDENT junk seqs to
47		// land mutually within one window; each is independent in 2^64, so that occurs
48		// with probability ~(window/2^64)^(C-1) ~ 1e-32 for C=3 and window=2048 — junk
49		// never triggers a resync, while a real discontinuity resyncs after losing at
50		// most C-1 frames. Any in-band (near-current) frame resets the run, so ordinary
51		// traffic interspersed with rare junk never accumulates a false corroboration.
52		resyncCorroborate = 3
53	)
54	
55	// Item is one released inner datagram plus the outer source address of the DATA
56	// frame that carried it. The source travels with the payload so the multipath
57	// Bind can pin its single virtual endpoint to the FIRST delivered frame's
58	// source even when that frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
59	type Item struct {
60		Payload []byte
61		Src     netip.AddrPort
62	}
63	
64	// slot is one ring cell. A live cell (occupied) holds exactly one buffered DATA
65	// frame whose OuterSeq maps to this index; seq is retained so a stale cell (an
66	// already-drained seq that shares the index modulo window) is never mistaken for
67	// a live one.
68	type slot struct {
69		seq      uint64
70		src      netip.AddrPort
71		payload  []byte
72		occupied bool
73	}
74	
75	// Resequencer is a bounded-window, timeout-based receive resequencing buffer. It
76	// consumes DATA frames tagged with the multipath codec's OWN outer sequence
77	// number (frame.Data.OuterSeq — never WireGuard's inner counter) arriving out of
78	// order across paths, and releases the decoded inner datagrams in strictly
79	// ascending outer-seq order before they reach the WireGuard engine. This absorbs
80	// multipath reorder entirely in the outer layer so WG's RFC 6479 anti-replay
81	// filter (window 8128, see docs/p0-findings.md §6) only ever sees monotonically
82	// increasing inner counters and never drops a legitimately-delivered packet.
83	//
84	// Guarantees (see the package tests):
85	//   - Ordering: released frames are strictly ascending in outer-seq. A frame
86	//     whose seq is below the release point is dropped, never delivered late, so
87	//     delivery is monotonic and WG sees no replay-window regression.
88	//   - Exactly-once: duplicates (same seq re-observed before or after release) are
89	//     dropped; every deliverable frame is released exactly once.
90	//   - Bounded memory: at most `window` frames are buffered. A frame at or beyond
91	//     next+window forces the window to advance (releasing/skipping the tail),
92	//     so an adversarial reorder/loss trace cannot grow the buffer without bound.
93	//   - Timeout progress: when the head-of-line seq is missing, buffered frames
94	//     ahead of the gap are held at most `timeout`; then the gap is skipped
95	//     (treated as lost) and the run released, rather than stalling forever. Each
96	//     distinct head-of-line gap gets its OWN full timeout.
97	//   - Bounded compute: every Observe runs in O(window). No frame — however wild
98	//     its (unauthenticated, possibly forged) seq — can make the release point
99	//     advance one seq at a time across a gap of up to 2^64 under the mutex.
100	//   - Discontinuity resilience: an unauthenticated frame whose seq lies farther
101	//     than resyncFactor*window from next never moves the release point on its own.
102	//     A genuine peer restart or long outage — a run of frames carrying DISTINCT
103	//     seqs that mutually fall within one window — re-pins (resyncs) the release
104	//     point within a bounded number of frames; uniformly-random junk seqs, and a
105	//     single junk/forged seq re-delivered any number of times, do not.
106	//
107	// Concurrency: every method is guarded by one mutex, so the shared instance is
108	// safe for the multipath Bind's per-path receive goroutines. The mutex is held
109	// only for in-memory bookkeeping — never across a syscall — so it does not
110	// perturb the Bind's lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path or its
111	// syscall-outside-mutex send discipline.
112	//
113	// FEC seam (T24): Observe is the sole ingestion point and is keyed purely by
114	// outer-seq. An FEC decoder inserted on the receive path recovers missing DATA
115	// frames from PARITY and feeds each recovered frame's ORIGINAL outer-seq through
116	// Observe, identically to a natively-received frame — so FEC recovery slots in
117	// BEFORE resequencing with no change to this type.
118	type Resequencer struct {
119		window  uint64
120		timeout time.Duration
121		clock   Clock
122	
123		mu      sync.Mutex
124		started bool   // false until the first Observe pins the initial next
125		next    uint64 // lowest outer-seq not yet released
126		ring    []slot // len == window; indexed by seq % window
127		buf     int    // number of occupied slots
128	
129		ready    []Item // FIFO of released items awaiting Pop
130		readyPos int    // read cursor into ready (front of the FIFO)
131	
132		// Head-of-line timeout: when next is a gap but frames ahead are buffered,
133		// waiting is armed with a deadline measured from when the gap first formed.
134		waiting  bool
135		deadline time.Time
136	
137		// Discontinuity/resync guard: the current run of out-of-band (suspect) frames.
138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64
145		resyncHi   uint64
146	
147		// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
148		highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
149		dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
150		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
151		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
152		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout
153		releasedN   uint64 // frames released for delivery
154		resyncs     uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
155		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
156	}
157	
158	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
159	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
160	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
161	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
162	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
163		if window == 0 {
164			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
165		}
166		if clock == nil {
167			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
168		}
169		return &Resequencer{
170			window:  window,
171			timeout: timeout,
172			clock:   clock,
173			ring:    make([]slot, window),
174		}
175	}
176	
177	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
178	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
179	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
180	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
181	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
182	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
183		now := r.clock.Now()
184		r.mu.Lock()
185		defer r.mu.Unlock()
186	
187		if !r.started {
188			r.started = true
189			r.next = seq
190		}
191	
192		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
193		// where this frame lands.
194		r.expire(now)
195	
196		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
197		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
198		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
199		if !r.admit(seq) {
200			return
201		}
202	
203		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
204		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
205			r.dropDup++
206			return
207		}
208		cell.seq = seq
209		cell.src = src
210		cell.payload = payload
211		cell.occupied = true
212		r.buf++
213		if r.buf > r.highWater {
214			r.highWater = r.buf
215		}
216	
217		r.drain()
218		r.arm(now)
219	}
220	
221	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
222	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
223	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
224	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
225	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
226	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
227	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
228	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
229	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
230	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
231	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
232		now := r.clock.Now()
233		r.mu.Lock()
234		defer r.mu.Unlock()
235	
236		if !r.started {
237			r.started = true
238			r.next = seq
239		}
240		r.expire(now)
241	
242		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
243		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
244		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
245		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
246		// touches next or the corroboration run.
247		if seq < r.next {
248			if r.next-seq > r.window {
249				r.dropSuspect++
250			} else {
251				r.dropLate++
252			}
253			return false
254		}
255		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
256			r.dropLate++
257			return false
258		}
259	
260		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
261		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
262			r.dropDup++
263			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
264		}
265		cell.seq = seq
266		cell.src = src
267		cell.payload = payload
268		cell.occupied = true
269		r.buf++
270		if r.buf > r.highWater {
271			r.highWater = r.buf
272		}
273	
274		r.drain()
275		r.arm(now)
276		return true
277	}
278	
279	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
280	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
281	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
282	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a
283	// bounded amount on a plausible loss burst, or re-pin next on a corroborated
284	// discontinuity. Caller holds r.mu.
285	func (r *Resequencer) admit(seq uint64) bool {
286		if seq < r.next {
287			// Below the release point. A frame more than one window below next is
288			// impossibly late under bounded reorder — treat it as SUSPECT (a peer
289			// restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here). A frame within a
290			// window below next is an ordinary straggler/duplicate.
291			if r.next-seq > r.window {
292				if r.tryResync(seq) {
293					return true
294				}
295				r.dropSuspect++
296				return false
297			}
298			r.resyncReset() // near-current traffic: not a discontinuity
299			r.dropLate++
300			return false
301		}
302		// seq >= next.
303		if seq-r.next >= resyncFactor*r.window {
304			// Too far ahead to be a plausible loss burst — SUSPECT (garbage-decoded or
305			// forged high seq). A single such frame must not advance next.
306			if r.tryResync(seq) {
307				return true
308			}
309			r.dropSuspect++
310			return false
311		}
312		// In-window, or a moderate (plausible loss-burst) forward jump: legitimate
313		// near-current traffic, so any in-progress discontinuity run is broken.
314		r.resyncReset()
315		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
316			// Beyond the window: advance the release point so the frame fits at the top
317			// of the window, releasing buffered frames below the new base in order and
318			// skipping the (assumed lost) gaps. This is what bounds memory.
319			r.advanceTo(seq - r.window + 1)
320		}
321		return true
322	}
323	
324	// Pop returns the next in-order released inner datagram, or ok=false when none is
325	// ready. It also advances the head-of-line timeout, so a receive goroutine that
326	// only drains still makes timeout progress.
327	func (r *Resequencer) Pop() (Item, bool) {
328		now := r.clock.Now()
329		r.mu.Lock()
330		defer r.mu.Unlock()
331	
332		r.expire(now)
333	
334		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
335			return Item{}, false
336		}
337		it := r.ready[r.readyPos]
338		r.ready[r.readyPos] = Item{} // release the payload reference
339		r.readyPos++
340		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
341			// FIFO drained: reset to reuse the backing array, keeping it bounded.
342			r.ready = r.ready[:0]
343			r.readyPos = 0
344		}
345		return it, true
346	}
347	
348	// drain releases the contiguous run starting at next into the ready FIFO. Caller
349	// holds r.mu.
350	func (r *Resequencer) drain() {
351		for {
352			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
353			if !cell.occupied || cell.seq != r.next {
354				return
355			}
356			r.release(cell)
357			r.next++
358		}
359	}
360	
361	// advanceTo moves next up to target, releasing any occupied cell it passes (in
362	// ascending order) and counting the empty positions as skipped (lost) seqs. It is
363	// O(window), NEVER O(target-next): all occupied cells live in [next, next+window),
364	// so once next reaches next+window the remaining gap is provably empty and is
365	// closed by arithmetic rather than iterated. Caller holds r.mu; target must be
366	// > next.
367	func (r *Resequencer) advanceTo(target uint64) {
368		// Only [next, next+window) can hold occupied cells; iterate at most that far.
369		limit := target
370		if limit > r.next+r.window {
371			limit = r.next + r.window
372		}
373		for r.next < limit {
374			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
375			if cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next {
376				r.release(cell)
377			} else {
378				r.skipped++
379			}
380			r.next++
381		}
382		if r.next < target {
383			// The remaining gap [next, target) is entirely empty — no occupied cell can
384			// live a full window ahead of the old release point. Close it by arithmetic.
385			r.skipped += target - r.next
386			r.next = target
387		}
388	}
389	
390	// release moves one occupied cell's payload into the ready FIFO and clears it.
391	// Caller holds r.mu.
392	func (r *Resequencer) release(cell *slot) {
393		r.ready = append(r.ready, Item{Payload: cell.payload, Src: cell.src})
394		cell.occupied = false
395		cell.payload = nil
396		r.buf--
397		r.releasedN++
398	}
399	
400	// expire skips a head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed: it jumps next to
401	// the smallest buffered seq (treating the intervening seqs as lost) and releases
402	// the now-contiguous run. Caller holds r.mu.
403	func (r *Resequencer) expire(now time.Time) {
404		if !r.waiting || now.Before(r.deadline) {
405			return
406		}
407		minSeq, ok := r.smallestBuffered()
408		if !ok {
409			r.waiting = false
410			return
411		}
412		// Skip the lost gap [next, minSeq), then release from minSeq onward.
413		for r.next < minSeq {
414			r.skipped++
415			r.next++
416		}
417		r.drain()
418		// Clear the armed state BEFORE re-arming so that a distinct SECOND gap exposed
419		// by this release gets its own full timeout rather than inheriting this gap's
420		// already-elapsed deadline (which would skip it with ~zero hold).
421		r.waiting = false
422		r.arm(now)
423	}
424	
425	// arm (re)evaluates the head-of-line timeout after a change. When next is a gap
426	// but buffered frames sit ahead of it, it starts the hold clock (only if not
427	// already running, so the deadline measures from when the gap first formed).
428	// Otherwise it disarms. Caller holds r.mu.
429	func (r *Resequencer) arm(now time.Time) {
430		cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
431		headPresent := cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next
432		if !headPresent && r.buf > 0 {
433			if !r.waiting {
434				r.waiting = true
435				r.deadline = now.Add(r.timeout)
436			}
437		} else {
438			r.waiting = false
439		}
440	}
441	
442	// tryResync feeds one out-of-band (suspect) seq into the discontinuity guard. It
443	// re-pins the release point ONLY after resyncCorroborate DISTINCT suspect seqs
444	// whose values mutually span less than one window (a genuine peer restart or long
445	// outage emits connected, distinct seqs that corroborate; uniformly-random junk
446	// seqs, each independent in 2^64, do not, and a single junk/forged seq re-delivered
447	// contributes only ONE distinct value so it can never self-corroborate). It
448	// returns true and performs the resync when the corroboration threshold is met —
449	// re-pinning next to the triggering seq so the caller places it as the new head
450	// and delivery resumes immediately, without a phantom gap or an extra timeout —
451	// and false while still collecting or when the seq fails to corroborate the
452	// current run. Caller holds r.mu.
453	func (r *Resequencer) tryResync(seq uint64) bool {
454		if len(r.resyncSeqs) == 0 || r.spanExceeds(seq) {
455			// Start (or restart) the run at this seq: it does not corroborate the
456			// current run, so it becomes the anchor of a fresh one.
457			r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi = seq, seq
458			r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs[:0], seq)
459			return false
460		}
461		if r.runContains(seq) {
462			// A repeated identical seq does NOT advance corroboration: a lone junk or
463			// forged datagram re-delivered must not count as independent corroboration.
464			return false
465		}
466		if seq < r.resyncLo {
467			r.resyncLo = seq
468		}
469		if seq > r.resyncHi {
470			r.resyncHi = seq
471		}
472		r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs, seq)
473		if len(r.resyncSeqs) < resyncCorroborate {
474			return false
475		}
476		// Corroborated discontinuity: re-pin to the triggering seq and resume delivery.
477		r.resync(seq)
478		return true
479	}
480	
481	// runContains reports whether seq is already one of the distinct suspect seqs in
482	// the current corroboration run. The run holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so
483	// this scan is O(C). Caller holds r.mu.
484	func (r *Resequencer) runContains(seq uint64) bool {
485		for _, s := range r.resyncSeqs {
486			if s == seq {
487				return true
488			}
489		}
490		return false
491	}
492	
493	// spanExceeds reports whether adding seq to the current corroboration run would
494	// make its seq span reach or exceed one window (i.e. seq does not mutually fall
495	// within one window of the run so far). Caller holds r.mu.
496	func (r *Resequencer) spanExceeds(seq uint64) bool {
497		lo, hi := r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi
498		if seq < lo {
499			lo = seq
500		}
501		if seq > hi {
502			hi = seq
503		}
504		return hi-lo >= r.window
505	}
506	
507	// resync re-pins the release point to base after a confirmed discontinuity,
508	// discarding all buffered frames (they belong to the pre-discontinuity stream) in
509	// O(window). The already-released FIFO is untouched — those were legitimate prior
510	// deliveries. Caller holds r.mu.
511	func (r *Resequencer) resync(base uint64) {
512		for i := range r.ring {
513			r.ring[i] = slot{}
514		}
515		r.buf = 0
516		r.next = base
517		r.waiting = false
518		r.resyncReset()
519		r.resyncs++
520	}
521	
522	// resyncReset abandons the current corroboration run. Caller holds r.mu.
523	func (r *Resequencer) resyncReset() {
524		r.resyncSeqs = r.resyncSeqs[:0]
525		r.resyncLo = 0
526		r.resyncHi = 0
527	}
528	
529	// Rebaseline discards the buffered (pre-switch) frames and UNPINS the release
530	// point so the NEXT Observe re-anchors `next` to that frame's outer-seq, exactly as
531	// if the stream had just started. It exists for the edge-side HUB FAILOVER (T57): a
532	// concentrator switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER, and the standby is a separate
533	// process whose outer-seq restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
534	// hub's high-rate stream advanced `next` to. The unauthenticated tryResync path
535	// cannot rescue this in time: it needs resyncCorroborate (3) distinct low seqs
536	// within one window, but a freshly re-handshaking standby emits only ~1 DATA frame
537	// (the handshake response) per RekeyTimeout, so corroboration falls outside the
538	// failover window and the response is dropped as SUSPECT — the tunnel never
539	// re-establishes (defect D32). Unlike a DATA frame (forgeable by design, hence the
540	// corroboration guard), THIS re-pin is driven by a TRUSTED control event — the
541	// operator-configured ordered-endpoint switch, above the unauthenticated wire — so
542	// re-anchoring on the next single frame is sound. Buffered frames (from the dead
543	// prior hub) are discarded in O(window); the already-released FIFO of prior
544	// legitimate deliveries is untouched. Idempotent and safe before the first Observe
545	// (started stays false). Takes r.mu; the caller must NOT hold it.
546	func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
547		r.mu.Lock()
548		defer r.mu.Unlock()
549		for i := range r.ring {
550			r.ring[i] = slot{}
551		}
552		r.buf = 0
553		r.started = false
554		r.waiting = false
555		r.resyncReset()
556		r.rebaselines++
557	}
558	
559	// smallestBuffered scans the ring for the lowest occupied seq at or above next.
560	// All occupied seqs lie in [next, next+window), so this is bounded by window and
561	// runs only on the (rare) timeout path. Caller holds r.mu.
562	func (r *Resequencer) smallestBuffered() (uint64, bool) {
563		best := uint64(0)
564		found := false
565		for i := range r.ring {
566			c := &r.ring[i]
567			if c.occupied && c.seq >= r.next && (!found || c.seq < best) {
568				best = c.seq
569				found = true
570			}
571		}
572		return best, found
573	}
574	
575	// Buffered reports the number of frames currently held (not yet released). It is
576	// always <= window, which the bounded-memory tests assert.
577	func (r *Resequencer) Buffered() int {
578		r.mu.Lock()
579		defer r.mu.Unlock()
580		return r.buf
581	}
582	
583	// Pending reports the number of released-but-not-yet-popped items in the FIFO.
584	func (r *Resequencer) Pending() int {
585		r.mu.Lock()
586		defer r.mu.Unlock()
587		return len(r.ready) - r.readyPos
588	}
589	
590	// HighWater reports the maximum number of frames ever buffered simultaneously.
591	func (r *Resequencer) HighWater() int {
592		r.mu.Lock()
593		defer r.mu.Unlock()
594		return r.highWater
595	}
596	
597	// Stats is a snapshot of the resequencer's cumulative counters.
598	type Stats struct {
599		Released       uint64 // frames released for delivery
600		DroppedDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
601		DroppedOld     uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
602		DroppedSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
603		Skipped        uint64 // seqs skipped (lost) by window-advance or timeout
604		Resyncs        uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
605		Rebaselines    uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
606	}
607	
608	// Stats returns a snapshot of the cumulative counters.
609	func (r *Resequencer) Stats() Stats {
610		r.mu.Lock()
611		defer r.mu.Unlock()
612		return Stats{
613			Released:       r.releasedN,
614			DroppedDup:     r.dropDup,
615			DroppedOld:     r.dropLate,
616			DroppedSuspect: r.dropSuspect,
617			Skipped:        r.skipped,
618			Resyncs:        r.resyncs,
619			Rebaselines:    r.rebaselines,
620		}
621	}
622

3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"testing"
6	)

8	// TestSetPeerRemoteRepointsEveryPath is the bind half of the T57 hub-failover switch:
9	// SetPeerRemote must repoint EVERY path's wire remote at the standby concentrator —
10	// OVERRIDING an already-learned remote — and update the default remote so a subsequent
11	// Open seeds fresh paths at the standby. This is a DELIBERATE contrast with ParseEndpoint,
12	// which only fills a path's remote when it is UNSET; a hub switch, by contrast, retargets
13	// the whole bond, so it must override. Seeding both paths with a distinct "active" remote
14	// first makes the assertion non-vacuous: it fails on any implementation that skips a path
15	// that already has a remote (the ParseEndpoint semantics).
16	func TestSetPeerRemoteRepointsEveryPath(t *testing.T) {
17		psk := testKey(t, 0x5A)
18		m, err := newMultipath(t, loopbackPaths(2), psk)
19		if err != nil {
20			t.Fatalf("NewMultipath: %v", err)
21		}
22		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
23			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
24		}
25		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
26	
27		// Both paths have already learned/configured a remote toward the ACTIVE concentrator.
28		active := netip.MustParseAddrPort("203.0.113.1:51820")
29		for _, ps := range m.paths {
30			ps.setRemote(active)
31		}
32	
33		// Hub loss → switch the peer remote to the STANDBY concentrator.
34		standby := netip.MustParseAddrPort("198.51.100.7:51820")
35		m.SetPeerRemote(standby)
36	
37		for i, ps := range m.paths {
38			got, ok := ps.getRemote()
39			if !ok || got != standby {
40				t.Fatalf("path %d remote = %v (known=%v), want standby %v (SetPeerRemote must override an existing remote)", i, got, ok, standby)
41			}
42		}
43	
44		// The default remote is updated too, so the next Open seeds fresh paths at the standby.
45		m.mu.Lock()
46		dr, has := m.defaultRemote, m.hasDefaultRemote
47		m.mu.Unlock()
48		if !has || dr != standby {
49			t.Fatalf("defaultRemote = %v (set=%v), want %v", dr, has, standby)
50		}
51	}

1520	// engine applies UAPI config before binding), so the parsed address is stashed
1521	// and also applied to any already-open path lacking its own dest_addr.
1522	func (m *Multipath) ParseEndpoint(s string) (Endpoint, error) {
1523		ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
1524		if err != nil {
1525			return nil, err
1526		}
1527		m.mu.Lock()
1528		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1529		m.defaultRemote, m.hasDefaultRemote = ap, true
1530		if !m.virt.dstValid() {
1531			m.virt.setDst(ap)
1532		}
1533		for _, ps := range m.paths {
1534			if _, ok := ps.getRemote(); !ok {
1535				ps.setRemote(ap)
1536			}
1537		}
1538		return m.virt, nil
1539	}
1540	
1541	// SetPeerRemote repoints EVERY path's wire remote — and the default remote seeded onto
1542	// paths without their own dest_addr — at ap, the concentrator endpoint the edge now
1543	// sends to. It is the edge-side HUB-FAILOVER switch (T57): when every path's liveness to
1544	// the active concentrator is DOWN (hub loss), the device advances to the next ordered
1545	// peer endpoint (config.Peer.Endpoints) and calls this so every subsequent DATA and
1546	// PROBE frame egresses toward the STANDBY concentrator on every path.
1547	//
1548	// It repoints UNIFORMLY — overriding a path's own configured dest_addr too — because a
1549	// concentrator switch retargets the peer for the whole bond: the ordered endpoint list
1550	// is a per-CONCENTRATOR list (one address per hub), so on failover every path must reach
1551	// the same standby hub. (A per-path dest_addr addresses the ACTIVE concentrator's
1552	// multi-address fronting; a hub switch supersedes it. All hubs in the ordered set share
1553	// the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the same session identity re-handshakes
1554	// against whichever hub is active.)
1555	//
1556	// It does NOT touch the engine's single virtual endpoint (invariant A1): the engine
1557	// still sees one peer, and only the per-path fan-out BENEATH it is repointed — no
1558	// per-packet endpoint churn reaches the engine. Fresh-session semantics (dropping the
1559	// old hub's keypairs, no hub-to-hub state handoff) and the re-handshake initiation are
1560	// the device's job, driven right after this call.
1561	//
1562	// Concurrency mirrors ParseEndpoint: it takes m.mu to publish the new default and
1563	// repoint each path's remote (each ps.setRemote takes the path's own mutex). It is
1564	// safe on a CLOSED bind (no paths) — the new default remote is still recorded, so the
1565	// next Open seeds fresh paths with it.
1566	func (m *Multipath) SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort) {
1567		m.mu.Lock()
1568		m.defaultRemote, m.hasDefaultRemote = ap, true
1569		for _, ps := range m.paths {
1570			ps.setRemote(ap)
1571		}
1572		rq := m.resequencer.Load()
1573		m.mu.Unlock()
1574	
1575		// A hub switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER identity: the standby concentrator is a
1576		// separate process whose outer-seq restarts near 1, far below the release point the
1577		// prior hub's stream advanced the shared resequencer's `next` to. Re-baseline it so the
1578		// standby's FIRST frame (the WG handshake response) re-anchors the release point instead
1579		// of being dropped as a suspect low seq — without this the tunnel never re-establishes
1580		// after failover (defect D32). Done OUTSIDE m.mu (the resequencer has its own mutex;
1581		// never nest it under m.mu). Nil on a closed bind (a resequencer is Stored per Open) —
1582		// the next Open seeds a fresh one whose release point re-pins to its first frame anyway.
1583		if rq != nil {
1584			rq.Rebaseline()
1585		}
1586	}
1587	
1588	// Close tears down every per-path socket and CLEARS the bind's path state so a
1589	// subsequent Open fully rebuilds it. The amneziawg engine drives exactly this
1590	// lifecycle — device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked calls Close() before
1591	// every Open(), and a Down/Up cycles Close after Open — so Close must leave the
1592	// bind reopenable (matching conn.StdNetBind, whose closed state is simply "no
1593	// sockets"). Outstanding receive calls return an error as their socket closes.
1594	func (m *Multipath) Close() error {
1595		m.mu.Lock()
1596		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1597		// Release the fan-in first so any reader parked on a delivery poke, and the
1598		// engine-facing drainer, unblock; then close the sockets so every readLoop
1599		// returns; then wait for them all to exit before clearing state so no reader
1600		// outlives this Close (and no reader touches the resequencer a later Open
1601		// replaces). readLoop needs no lock, so waiting under m.mu cannot deadlock.
1602		if m.recvClosed != nil {
1603			close(m.recvClosed)
1604		}
1605		err := m.closeSocketsLocked()
1606		m.readersWG.Wait()
1607		m.deliverSignal = nil
1608		m.recvClosed = nil
1609		return err
1610	}
1611	
1612	// closeSocketsLocked closes all path sockets and resets the bind to the unopened
1613	// state (paths and send Codec cleared), returning the first close error. Caller
1614	// holds m.mu. Idempotent: safe on an already-closed or never-opened bind — which
1615	// is exactly what the engine's pre-open Close relies on.
1616	func (m *Multipath) closeSocketsLocked() error {
1617		var firstErr error
1618		// Sockets are SHARED (one per shared path), so close them once from the shared list —
1619		// NOT once per (peer,path) view, which would double-close a concentrator socket.
1620		for _, sp := range m.shared {
1621			if sp.conn != nil {
1622				if err := sp.conn.Close(); err != nil && firstErr == nil {
1623					firstErr = err
1624				}
1625			}
1626		}
1627		m.shared = nil
1628		m.deferred = nil
1629		// Clear every peer's per-Open state so the next Open rebuilds it fresh (paths, send
1630		// Codec, FEC send/receive). The deadline-tick goroutine that reads a peer's fecSend is
1631		// stopped by Close (recvClosed) and joined via readersWG before state is cleared, and it
1632		// captured its own fecSender pointer, so niling here never races it.
1633		for _, p := range m.peers {
1634			p.paths = nil
1635			p.sendCodec = nil
1636			p.fecSend = nil
1637			p.fecRecv.Store(nil)
1638		}
1639		return firstErr

3	import (
4		"testing"

6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
7	)
8	
9	// TestPeerStatePerPSKCodecAndReflector is the T84 acceptance: two peerStates built through the
10	// per-peer construction seam (newPeerState) from DISTINCT psks derive independent frame Codecs
11	// and probe Reflectors. One peer's authenticated frame is rejected by the other's Decode
12	// (cross-psk frames are rejected on the MAC), and each Reflector authenticates a probe ONLY
13	// under its own psk. This is the property the concentrator relies on: PSK is a per-peer
14	// attribute now, not a bond-wide singleton, so a frame minted under peer A's key cannot be
15	// mistaken for peer B's.
16	func TestPeerStatePerPSKCodecAndReflector(t *testing.T) {
17		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
18		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
19	
20		// The scheduler/prober collaborators are irrelevant to codec/reflector derivation, so this
21		// exercises the PSK seam in isolation (nil is never dereferenced by newPeerState).
22		peerA := newPeerState("peer-a", pskA, nil, nil, nil)
23		peerB := newPeerState("peer-b", pskB, nil, nil, nil)
24	
25		// --- Codec: a frame encoded under peer A's psk fails peer B's Decode. ---
26		encA, err := peerA.newCodec()
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("peer A encode codec: %v", err)
29		}
30		decA, err := peerA.newCodec()
31		if err != nil {
32			t.Fatalf("peer A decode codec: %v", err)
33		}
34		decB, err := peerB.newCodec()
35		if err != nil {
36			t.Fatalf("peer B decode codec: %v", err)
37		}
38	
39		// A PROBE frame is authenticated (MAC-covered under the peer's authKey). Peer A's own codec
40		// round-trips it back to an authentic probe; peer B's codec, keyed by a DIFFERENT psk, can
41		// NEVER recover it AS an authentic probe — reaching KindProbe requires both the obfuscated
42		// kind byte to survive AND the MAC to verify under authKeyB, which is cryptographically
43		// infeasible for a frame MAC'd under authKeyA. (Decode's exact rejection is ErrMalformed or
44		// ErrAuth depending on how the random nonce garbles the kind byte under the wrong obfKey, so
45		// the deterministic invariant asserted is "never accepted as a probe", not a specific error.)
46		wire, err := encA.Encode(nil, frame.Probe{PathID: 0, ProbeSeq: 7, TimestampNanos: 1234, SessionID: 0xABCD})
47		if err != nil {
48			t.Fatalf("peer A encode: %v", err)
49		}
50		gotA, err := decA.Decode(wire)
51		if err != nil {
52			t.Fatalf("peer A must decode a frame minted under its own psk: %v", err)
53		}
54		if gotA.Kind() != frame.KindProbe {
55			t.Fatalf("peer A decoded its own probe as kind %d, want KindProbe", gotA.Kind())
56		}
57		if gotB, err := decB.Decode(wire); err == nil && gotB.Kind() == frame.KindProbe {
58			t.Fatalf("peer B accepted peer A's authenticated probe under a different psk: %+v", gotB)
59		}
60	
61		// --- Reflector: a probe authenticates (and is reflected) ONLY under its own peer's psk. ---
62		probeA, err := frame.Encode(pskA, frame.Probe{PathID: 0, ProbeSeq: 1, TimestampNanos: 99, SessionID: 0x1})
63		if err != nil {
64			t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk A: %v", err)
65		}
66		if _, err := peerA.reflector.Reflect(probeA); err != nil {
67			t.Fatalf("peer A reflector must authenticate a probe under its own psk: %v", err)
68		}
69		// Under peer B's psk the probe never reflects: it fails the MAC, or (when the wrong obfKey
70		// garbles the kind into an unauthenticated one) decodes to a non-probe the Reflector refuses.
71		if _, err := peerB.reflector.Reflect(probeA); err == nil {
72			t.Fatal("peer B reflector reflected a probe minted under psk A (cross-psk probe must be rejected)")
73		}
74	}

25	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
26	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
27	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
28	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
29	
30	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
31	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
32	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
33	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
34	type hubHealth interface {
35		State() telemetry.PathState
36	}
37	
38	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
39	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
40	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
41	type peerRemote interface {
42		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
43	}
44	
45	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
46	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
47	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
48	type rehandshake func()
49	
50	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
51	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
52	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
53	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
54	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
55	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
56	// advances again.
57	//
58	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
59	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
60	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
61	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
62	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
63	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
64	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
65	// bounded retry, not a storm.
66	//
67	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
68	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
69	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
70	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
71	// endpoint form, per T54).
72	type hubFailover struct {
73		// endpoints is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint list (config.Peer.Endpoints):
74		// index 0 = active/primary at boot, the rest ordered standbys. Immutable after

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5		"fmt"
6		"sync"
7		"sync/atomic"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
13	)
14	
15	// FEC datapath integration (T24).
16	//
17	// The fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon engine (internal/fec) is wired into the multipath
18	// datapath as a SEND-side encoder and a RECEIVE-side decoder that composes with the
19	// T18 resequencer, T21 weighted scheduling, and T30 dynamic paths without regressing
20	// any of them. FEC is OFF unless a config carries an [fec] block (NewMultipath's
21	// fecCfg is nil otherwise), in which case the datapath is byte-for-byte the pre-T24
22	// behaviour.
23	//
24	// Wire mapping. The FEC data-shard bytes are OuterSeq || inner: the encoder codes
25	// parity over that, but the 8-byte outer-seq prefix is NOT transmitted — the DATA
26	// frame carries the outer-seq in its header and the inner datagram in its payload,
27	// and both sender and receiver reconstitute the shard bytes as
28	// header-seq || frame-payload. This keeps a recovered shard SELF-DESCRIBING (its
29	// reconstructed bytes carry its own outer-seq, so a frame reconstructed from parity
30	// resequences at its ORIGINAL outer-seq even when EVERY data frame of the group was
31	// lost) while adding only one wire byte per DATA frame (the shard index in the DATA
32	// header) and the group cardinality M per PARITY frame. See internal/frame.
33	//
34	// Concurrency. The encoder rides the Send path, already serialized under m.mu, plus
35	// a single deadline-tick goroutine that TryLocks m.mu (never blocking, so it can
36	// never deadlock Close's readersWG.Wait held under m.mu — the tickLivenessFromReceive
37	// discipline). The decoder is fed from the per-path readLoop goroutines (many
38	// concurrent), so it is guarded by its own mutex, disjoint from m.mu and from the
39	// resequencer's — matching the resequencer's lock discipline (never held across a
40	// syscall). Recovered frames are handed to the resequencer exactly like a
41	// natively-received frame, so FEC recovery slots in strictly BEFORE resequencing.
42	
43	// fecSeqPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian outer-seq prefix that heads each
44	// FEC data-shard's coded bytes. It is reconstructed from the DATA frame header on
45	// both ends and never travels the wire (see the wire mapping above).
46	const fecSeqPrefixLen = 8
47	
48	// maxFECDeadline is the authoritative upper bound on the FEC group-close deadline,
49	// coupling it to the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout (T24, defect #4). A group
50	// flushed by the deadline emits its parity `deadline` after the group opened; the
51	// reconstructed frames must reach the resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap
52	// (resequencerTimeout after the gap forms), or recovery is structurally too late — the
53	// gap is skipped first and the recovered frame dropped as past the release point. Held
54	// at half the resequencer timeout, the deadline leaves the other half for propagation
55	// and reconstruction. config validation enforces a matching load-time bound; this is
56	// the authoritative guard for any fec.Config built directly.
57	const maxFECDeadline = resequencerTimeout / 2
58	
59	// fecRetainGroups bounds the decoder's retained-group window (SetRetainWindow): a
60	// group more than this many group-ids behind the newest offered group is evicted,
61	// which is also when an incomplete group is finally accounted unrecoverable. It is
62	// sized well above the reorder/loss span a single group can straddle so a group is
63	// never evicted while its own shards are still legitimately in flight, yet small
64	// enough that an unrecoverable group is accounted promptly rather than after a
65	// 4096-group default. A group spans a handful of outer-seqs, so this comfortably
66	// exceeds the resequencerWindow's reordering budget in group units.
67	const fecRetainGroups = 512
68	
69	// fecResidualLossWindow is the trailing outer-seq width of the receive-side residual-
70	// loss estimator (T29): the post-FEC-recovery loss the P4 acceptance bounds. It is
71	// deliberately WIDER than the telemetry default (512) for two reasons: (1) resolution —
72	// the P4 bound is 0.5%, so the window must resolve finer than 1/512≈0.2%; 8192 gives
73	// ~0.012% granularity, well under the bound; (2) stability — averaging the residual over
74	// ~thousands of groups smooths the per-group binomial variance into a steady-state figure.
75	// It stays far below the resequencer window's reordering budget, and is wide enough that a
76	// recovered frame arriving a group or two after its gap still retroactively fills it, so
77	// genuinely-unrecovered seqs (whole-group and beyond-budget losses) are the only residual.
78	const fecResidualLossWindow = 8192
79	
80	// adaptiveControlInterval throttles how often the FEC tick loop folds a fresh loss
81	// sample into the adaptive controller (T29). It matches the probe cadence — the rate at
82	// which per-path loss estimates actually refresh — so the controller's EWMA sees ~one
83	// sample per probe interval as its tuning (internal/adaptivefec) assumes, regardless of
84	// the (possibly much smaller) FEC group-close deadline the tick loop runs at.
85	const adaptiveControlInterval = telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
86	
87	// fecSender is the send-side FEC state: the group-forming encoder (accessed only
88	// under the Bind's m.mu, on the Send path and the deadline-tick goroutine) plus the
89	// parity-overhead counters the /metrics exposition reports. The counters are
90	// atomics read lock-free at snapshot time, mirroring the T23 per-path byte counters.
91	type fecSender struct {
92		enc *fec.Encoder
93	
94		// ctrl is the adaptive-FEC controller (T29), nil in fixed-ratio mode. It is driven
95		// (Observe) and read (Parity) ONLY from under the Bind's m.mu — the FEC tick loop's
96		// throttled drive and, transitively, the Send path — so it needs no lock of its own,
97		// matching the encoder's single-writer discipline. lastControlTick/haveControlTick
98		// throttle the drive to adaptiveControlInterval and are likewise m.mu-guarded.
99		ctrl            *adaptivefec.Controller
100		lastControlTick time.Time
101		haveControlTick bool
102	
103		dataFrames   atomic.Uint64
104		dataBytes    atomic.Uint64
105		parityFrames atomic.Uint64
106		parityBytes  atomic.Uint64
107	}
108	
109	// fecReceiver is the receive-side FEC state: the recovery decoder guarded by its own
110	// mutex (the per-path readLoop goroutines feed it concurrently). deliveredRecovered is
111	// the HONEST recovery count for /metrics — data frames a reconstruction actually
112	// delivered to the resequencer AHEAD of its release point (not merely reconstructed:
113	// a frame rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and
114	// must not be counted, else /metrics overstates recovery). It is an atomic so the
115	// snapshot reads it lock-free.
116	type fecReceiver struct {
117		mu  sync.Mutex
118		dec *fec.Decoder
119	
120		deliveredRecovered atomic.Uint64
121	
122		// connLoss is the POST-FEC-RECOVERY residual-loss estimator (T29, the P4 signal):
123		// every natively-received DATA outer-seq AND every FEC-reconstructed outer-seq is
124		// Observed into it, so its Loss() fraction is exactly the connection-scoped loss that
125		// survived FEC — outer-seqs neither received nor recovered. It is internally
126		// synchronized (its own mutex), fed concurrently by the per-path readLoop goroutines
127		// exactly like the resequencer, and read lock-free-enough at scrape time.
128		connLoss *telemetry.ConnLoss
129	}
130	
131	// offer feeds one surviving shard into the decoder and returns any data payloads it
132	// reconstructed (never-received frames). It holds the decoder mutex only for the
133	// in-memory reconstruct — never across a syscall — so it never blocks the send path.
134	func (r *fecReceiver) offer(s fec.Shard) ([]fec.Recovered, error) {
135		r.mu.Lock()
136		defer r.mu.Unlock()
137		return r.dec.Offer(s)
138	}
139	
140	// stats snapshots the decoder's cumulative recovery counters under its mutex.
141	func (r *fecReceiver) stats() fec.DecoderStats {
142		r.mu.Lock()
143		defer r.mu.Unlock()
144		return r.dec.Stats()
145	}
146	
147	// FECStats is a consistent snapshot of the Bind's FEC counters (T24), the shape the
148	// metrics.Source adapter maps onto the connection-scoped /metrics FEC series.
149	// DataFrames is the send-side DATA-frame count (the fixed-ratio overhead's
150	// denominator); ParityFrames/ParityBytes are the send-side overhead (parity emitted);
151	// Recovered and Unrecoverable are the receive-side outcome (data shards reconstructed
152	// from parity, and data shards lost beyond repair capacity). FEC being
153	// connection-scoped, these carry no path label. The frame overhead the operator cares
154	// about is ParityFrames/DataFrames, which tends to ParityShards/DataShards (M/K) once
155	// groups fill; both are counted only for frames that actually reached the socket, so
156	// the ratio reflects wire cost actually spent.
157	type FECStats struct {
158		DataFrames    uint64
159		DataBytes     uint64
160		ParityFrames  uint64
161		ParityBytes   uint64
162		Recovered     uint64
163		Unrecoverable uint64
164		// ResidualLoss is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
165		// (T29): the share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from
166		// parity. It is the P4 acceptance signal — the loss FEC did not mask. Zero when FEC is
167		// disabled or no data has flowed.
168		ResidualLoss float64
169	}
170	
171	// fecShardPayload builds the FEC data-shard coded bytes for outer-seq seq over the
172	// opaque inner datagram: seq (8 BE) || inner. The sender codes parity over this; the
173	// receiver reconstitutes the identical bytes from the DATA frame header + payload so
174	// the decoder's shards match what the encoder coded.
175	func fecShardPayload(seq uint64, inner []byte) []byte {
176		b := make([]byte, fecSeqPrefixLen+len(inner))
177		binary.BigEndian.PutUint64(b, seq)
178		copy(b[fecSeqPrefixLen:], inner)
179		return b
180	}
181	
182	// splitFECShardPayload inverts fecShardPayload, returning the outer-seq and a fresh
183	// copy of the inner datagram. It fails fast on a shard too short to hold the prefix,
184	// which would indicate a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable
185	// condition. The inner copy lets the resequencer take ownership without retaining the
186	// larger coded-shard backing array.
187	func splitFECShardPayload(b []byte) (uint64, []byte, error) {
188		if len(b) < fecSeqPrefixLen {
189			return 0, nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: recovered FEC shard %d bytes, shorter than the %d-byte outer-seq prefix", len(b), fecSeqPrefixLen)
190		}
191		seq := binary.BigEndian.Uint64(b[:fecSeqPrefixLen])
192		inner := make([]byte, len(b)-fecSeqPrefixLen)
193		copy(inner, b[fecSeqPrefixLen:])
194		return seq, inner, nil
195	}

1340	// fecTickLoop drives the FEC encoder's grouping deadline (T24): a partially-filled
1341	// group whose size threshold has not been reached is closed on time so its parity is
1342	// emitted rather than stranded until the next data frame. It ticks at the configured
1343	// deadline period and exits on recvClosed. It is tracked by readersWG so Close waits
1344	// for it. Like tickLivenessFromReceive it only ever TryLocks m.mu, so it can never
1345	// deadlock Close's readersWG.Wait (which runs while Close holds m.mu).
1346	func (m *Multipath) fecTickLoop(period time.Duration, closed <-chan struct{}) {
1347		defer m.readersWG.Done()
1348		ticker := time.NewTicker(period)
1349		defer ticker.Stop()
1350		for {
1351			select {
1352			case <-closed:
1353				return
1354			case <-ticker.C:
1355				m.fecFlushDeadline()
1356			}
1357		}
1358	}
1359	
1360	// fecFlushDeadline closes any FEC group whose grouping deadline has elapsed and emits
1361	// its parity on a scheduler-chosen path. It TryLocks m.mu: when the lock is contended
1362	// (a concurrent Send/Close/AddPath) it simply skips this tick — the next tick, or the
1363	// next size-triggered close, still emits the group's parity, and skipping preserves
1364	// Close's invariant that no readersWG goroutine blocks on m.mu. Framing runs under the
1365	// lock (the shared stateful send Codec); the socket writes run without it, mirroring
1366	// Send.
1367	func (m *Multipath) fecFlushDeadline() {
1368		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
1369			return
1370		}
1371		if m.fecSend == nil || len(m.paths) == 0 {
1372			m.mu.Unlock()
1373			return
1374		}
1375		// Adaptive drive (T29): fold a fresh loss sample into the controller and retarget the
1376		// encoder's per-group parity BEFORE flushing, so a group closed by this tick already
1377		// carries the current target. It is a no-op in fixed mode and self-throttles to the
1378		// probe cadence. Runs under the same m.mu the flush holds — the single serialized FEC
1379		// locus — so the controller (a state machine) is never touched concurrently.
1380		m.driveAdaptiveControllerLocked()

+// (derived from THIS peer's psk), its receive resequencer, and — when FEC is configured —
+// its FEC send/receive planes. Each is fresh per Open so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect)
+// re-pins the peer's release point and re-anchors its FEC grouping to this bring-up rather
+// than a stale prior span. It writes ONLY the given peer's fields (from the peer's own psk
+// and the bind-wide fecCfg/adaptiveCfg), so one peer's (re)creation never touches another
+// peer's resequencer or FEC group state — the per-peer lifecycle boundary that keeps a
+// reconnect on one peer from disturbing another's. Caller holds m.mu; on error the caller
+// unwinds the whole Open via closeSocketsLocked (which clears every peer's per-Open state),
+// so a partial build here is cleaned up.

+  // goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu.

+    // Quiescence-accurate unrecoverable accounting (D24): fold a doomed group into
+    // the unrecoverable counter once its recovery could no longer have helped, even

+    // link, or traffic that simply stops after an incident). Past fecRecoveryDeadline

+    // arrived (encoder grouping deadline) AND the resequencer has certainly skipped
+    // its gap (resequencerTimeout), so any later reconstruction is delivered too late
